findings.title: The "4.45 MB page" is a parse/DOM-construction problem, not a transfer problem. Reframe before costing anything.
findings.evidence: Lighthouse resource-summary, plants-index.mobile.json: total transfer for /plants/ = 566,244 B over 16 requests, split Image 280,614 B (49.6%) / Font 140,087 B (24.7%) / **Document 136,752 B (24.2%)** / Stylesheet 8,791 B (1.6%). The 4,516,415-byte HTML ships as 136,752 B: LESS than the images and about the same as the three woff2 files. I reproduced the exact figure: node zlib brotli quality 5 on dist/plants/index.html = 135,887 B, matching static-analysis.json's `br` field exactly (q4=163,157, q6=131,452), so the site is served at brotli q5 and 135,887 B is the real wire cost. Regression over the 7 Lighthouse-audited pages (raw bytes from static-analysis.json vs mainthread-work-breakdown): parseHTML = 111.9 ms per raw MB + 9.6 ms, R^2 = 0.9990 (12/13/15/120/170/322/485 ms across 0.06 to 4.31 MB). 'other' (DOM construction/GC) = 83.1 ms/MB, R^2 = 0.8227. scriptEvaluation = 28.0 ms/MB, R^2 = 0.9622. TOTAL main thread = 215.1 ms/MB, R^2 = 0.8629. /plants/ mobile spends 485 ms parseHTML + 506 ms 'other' = 991 ms of its 1,443 ms main thread on turning 4.4 MB of raw HTML into 89,370 nodes.
findings.impact: If the plan is costed as a bandwidth fix it will be judged on the wrong metric and look like a waste (135 KB is already fine). What the user actually pays is ~1.0 s of mobile main-thread time before the page is interactive, TBT 201 ms, and a DOM so large the Lighthouse accessibility gatherer dies (PROTOCOL_TIMEOUT on all 40+ a11y audits in plants-index.mobile.json). The correct success metric is raw bytes and DOM nodes, priced at ~215 ms of mobile main thread per raw MB removed.
findings.suggestedFix: State the goal as "cut raw HTML bytes and DOM nodes on card-grid pages", with the conversion rate ~112 ms parseHTML (and ~215 ms total main thread) per raw MB removed on a 4x-throttled mobile CPU. Do not promise transfer savings: the biggest available wire win on the whole page is 19.8 KB (see dead-data-attrs), and one of the proposed fixes actually makes transfer worse.

findings.title: data-tags and data-cat are emitted on all 1,677 cards on every card-grid page and have ZERO consumers anywhere in the repo
findings.evidence: src/components/PlantCard.astro:57 emits `data-tags={tagsAttr} data-cat={catAttr}`. Grep across src/ for `data-tags|data-cat|dataset.tags|dataset.cat|[data-tags|[data-cat` returns only the emitting lines: no reader in any script, and no CSS selector uses them (the only data-* CSS selector is `.card[data-name] .body` at src/styles/global.css:239). Measured on dist/plants/index.html: data-tags = 1,677 occurrences, 110,361 raw bytes (2.4% of the page); data-cat = 1,677 occurrences, 30,517 raw bytes (0.7%). Removing just those two: raw 4,411 KB -> 4,273 KB (-3.1%) and brotli(q5) 132.7 KB -> 117.5 KB (**-11.4%**). Same on /plants/zone/5/: data-tags = 70,075 raw bytes, -10.4% brotli on its own. data-tags is high-entropy free text ("shrub full sun summer fall white drought tolerant"), which is exactly what brotli cannot dedupe, so it dominates the compressed page.
findings.impact: The single largest transfer win available on the entire site, and the cheapest. -11.4% of the served bytes of /plants/ and every hub and bloom-calendar combo page, for deleting two attributes. Also ~138 KB of raw parse on /plants/ (~-30 ms mobile main thread by the 215 ms/MB rate).
findings.suggestedFix: Delete `data-tags={tagsAttr}` and `data-cat={catAttr}` from src/components/PlantCard.astro:57, and the now-unused `tagsAttr` (line 13) and `catAttr` (line 15) consts. Nothing reads them. If they are being held as a hook for a future client-side tag filter, that is a decision to make explicitly, not by default, and it should be gated on the same prop as the other filter attrs (see hub-pages-ship-dead-filter-attrs).

findings.title: Hub and bloom-calendar pages carry all 8 data-* filter attributes but have no filter script at all
findings.evidence: src/pages/plants/index.astro is the ONLY page in src/pages/ that ships a filter (grep for `getElementById("f-q")` / `querySelectorAll("#plant-results .card")` matches that file only). Every hub (src/pages/plants/zone/[zone].astro, color/[color], light/[sun], traits/[tag], type/[category], blooming-in/[month]) and every bloom-calendar combo renders cards through HubBody.astro:25 -> PlantGrid.astro:13 -> PlantCard.astro, which unconditionally emits all 8 data-* attrs (PlantCard.astro:56-58). Confirmed in the build: dist/plants/zone/5/index.html contains 1,070 data-tags and 1,070 data-months attributes and no code that reads them. Measured on /plants/zone/5/: stripping all seven non-name data-* attrs (keeping data-name, which src/styles/global.css:239 `.card[data-name] .body` needs) gives raw 2,803 KB -> 2,611 KB (-6.8%) and brotli(q5) 85.7 KB -> 68.7 KB (**-19.8%**). Same measurement on /plants/index.html gives -6.7% raw / -20.0% brotli, but /plants/ genuinely needs six of them.
findings.impact: Roughly a fifth of the served bytes of every hub page and every bloom-calendar combo page is attribute data that nothing can ever read. That is ~98 pages (static-analysis.json: 5 pages >3MB, 40 at 1-3MB, 35 at 500KB-1MB), i.e. every page a search engine is most likely to land a user on.
findings.suggestedFix: Add an opt-in prop, e.g. `interface Props { plant: Plant; eager?: boolean; filterable?: boolean }` on PlantCard.astro (default false) and a matching pass-through on PlantGrid.astro. Emit data-colors/zones/months/sun/photos (and data-tags/data-cat if they are ever wired up) only when `filterable`. Always emit data-name: global.css:239 depends on it as a selector. Set `filterable` only from src/pages/plants/index.astro:58. HubBody.astro needs no change (it just does not pass the prop). This is a purely additive prop, so no hub page markup shifts except the attribute drop.

findings.title: The known issue's <symbol> sprite is real but is a parse-cost fix: -24.8% raw bytes, only -1.5% wire and -1.9% DOM nodes
findings.evidence: Disjoint measurement of dist/plants/index.html: the four inline spec icons (PlantCard.astro:47-52, rendered at PlantCard.astro:79) are 1,538,363 bytes = **34.1% of the whole page**, across 6,704 <svg> elements. I simulated the sprite on the real built HTML (replacing every spec svg with `<svg class="ic" aria-hidden="true"><use href="#i-sun"/></svg>` plus one inline <symbol> block): raw 4,411 KB -> 3,319 KB (**-24.8%**), brotli(q5) 132.7 -> 130.7 KB (only **-1.5%**), elements 89,233 -> 87,572 (only **-1.9%**). The node saving is small because a <use> still creates 2 elements (svg + use) where the current markup creates 2-3 (svg + path, or svg + path + circle for the sun icon): net exactly 1 element per card, so 1,677 nodes on /plants/. The <use> shadow tree does not count toward the document element total. The wire saving is small because brotli already deduplicates four icon strings repeated 1,677 times almost perfectly.
findings.impact: Worth doing, but for the right reason: -1,092 KB of raw HTML on /plants/ is ~-122 ms of mobile parseHTML at the measured 111.9 ms/MB (485 ms -> ~363 ms), and ~-235 ms of total main thread at 215 ms/MB. It will NOT move the Lighthouse dom-size number and will NOT meaningfully cut transfer. Costing it as a "cut the 4.45 MB page" win will disappoint.
findings.suggestedFix: Hoist the six ICONS from PlantCard.astro:46-53 into one inline <symbol> sprite emitted once per page (a component rendered from Layout.astro, or once at the top of PlantGrid.astro), and reference them with `<svg class="ic" aria-hidden="true"><use href="#i-{f.k}"/></svg>`. The sprite MUST be inlined in the document, not an external /sprite.svg: the icons use stroke="currentColor" and global.css:222 sets `.card .spec svg { color: var(--bs-accent-text) }`, and cross-document <use> does not reliably inherit currentColor in Safari. Existing CSS (`.card .spec svg { width:13px; height:13px }` at global.css:222, and the 10px override at global.css:242) still matches the outer <svg>, so no CSS change is needed. Verify the sprite <svg> container is outside .card so `.card .spec svg` cannot match it.

findings.title: The known issue's gradient-painted bloom strip is a DOM-node win that MAKES TRANSFER WORSE and regresses light-theme visuals
findings.evidence: Simulated on the real built HTML (12 `` per card replaced by one element carrying a 12-hard-stop linear-gradient): raw 4,411 -> 4,233 KB (only **-4.0%**), elements 89,233 -> 70,786 (**-20.7%**), but brotli(q5) 132.7 -> 135.3 KB (**+2.0%, i.e. WORSE**). Head-to-head on top of the other fixes: S+M+D (sprite + months + dead attrs) = 114.7 KB brotli / 67,448 elements; S+G+M+D (same, plus gradient) = 117.5 KB brotli / 49,001 elements. The gradient therefore costs +2.8 KB of wire to buy -18,447 DOM nodes. Reason: the 12 cells today are `` (32 bytes, 14,999 of the 20,124 cells carry NO colour at all and compress to nearly nothing), whereas a per-card gradient string is ~180 unique bytes brotli cannot fold away. Visual regression: global.css:168 gives each cell `border-radius: 2px`, global.css:165 a `gap: 2px`, and global.css:183 gives every cell in LIGHT theme `box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 1px rgba(21,32,25,.20)`. A single linear-gradient can fake the gap but cannot reproduce a per-cell radius or a per-cell inset outline, so the light-theme strip would flatten from 12 rounded outlined chips to a banded bar. Accessibility contract DOES survive: role="img" + aria-label live on div.bloomstrip (BloomStrip.astro:16 and :23), never on the cells, and the label spells the range out in words (BloomStrip.astro:11), so the cells are already presentational children of a role=img.
findings.impact: CLAUDE.md's "Known issues" pairs this with the sprite as though both cut the page. It does not cut the page: it cuts nodes and adds bytes. It is still defensible (2.8 KB for 18k nodes is a fine trade) but only if the light-theme chip styling is deliberately given up, which is a design decision, not a perf cleanup.
findings.suggestedFix: If taken: paint the gradient with `transparent` for non-bloom months layered over `background: var(--bs-track)` so the empty-track colour stays theme-driven (a baked hex would break the dark/light themes, since today empty cells carry no inline colour and get theirs from global.css:168 / :183). Then delete the `:root[data-theme="light"] .bloomstrip .cell` rule (global.css:183) and accept the flat band, or reproduce the outline once on the container. Requires a light-theme AND dark-theme screenshot of a card grid before it ships. Honest alternative: skip the gradient and take the months row instead (below), which yields a similar node saving, saves raw bytes instead of costing wire bytes, and has no styling to reproduce.

findings.title: The win the known issue misses: the 12 month-letter spans, repeated 1,677 times, are 22.6% of the page's DOM and are aria-hidden
findings.evidence:
BloomStrip.astro:19 and :26 render `
{MONTHS.map((m) => {m[0]})}
`: 12 identical single-letter spans per card, byte-for-byte the same on every one of the 1,677 cards. Measured on dist/plants/index.html: 367,263 raw bytes (8.1% of the page) and 20,124 elements. Simulated replacement with a single empty div: raw 4,411 -> 4,135 KB (-6.2%), elements 89,233 -> 69,109 (**-22.6%**), brotli neutral (132.7 -> 132.4 KB). That is a LARGER DOM saving than the gradient bloom strip (-20.7%) and 12x larger than the sprite (-1.9%), and unlike the gradient it costs no wire bytes. It carries zero accessibility contract: the row is already `aria-hidden="true"`, and the bloom range is spelled out in words in the strip's aria-label (BloomStrip.astro:11). Per-card DOM budget for context: 53 elements, of which 12 bloom cells + 12 month letters = 24, i.e. 45% of every card's DOM is the bloom-calendar widget.
findings.impact: 20,124 elements on /plants/, 12,840 on /plants/zone/5/, and proportionally on all ~98 heavy pages, spent on decoration that is invisible to assistive tech and identical on every card. Combined with the sprite and the dead attrs it takes /plants/ from 89,233 to 67,447 elements and 4,411 to 2,906 KB raw (-34%), which is ~-165 ms of mobile parseHTML.
findings.suggestedFix: There is NO zero-risk one-liner here; pick deliberately. (a) Paint the letters from CSS on a single element: a data-URI SVG used as `mask-image` with `background: var(--bs-faint)` keeps it theme-aware, but the letters must be OUTLINED TO PATHS in the data-URI because a data-URI SVG cannot load the page webfont (Hanken Grotesk) and would otherwise fall back to a generic face. Alignment must match `grid-template-columns: repeat(12, 1fr); gap: 2px` (global.css:176). Medium effort, needs a screenshot. (b) Drop the compact months row entirely on card grids (keep it on the plant-detail 'full' variant, BloomStrip.astro:19): trivial, but sighted users lose the month scale under the mini strip, so it is a design call. Do NOT try `content: "JFMAMJJASOND"` on a pseudo-element: a single string cannot be distributed across 12 grid columns.

findings.title: Even with every proposed fix applied, /plants/ still has 49k-67k DOM nodes. Markup slimming does not fix "too big for tooling".
findings.evidence: Simulated on the real built HTML, all fixes combined: sprite + months-to-CSS + dead-attr drop = 89,233 -> 67,447 elements (-24.4%), raw 4,411 -> 2,906 KB (-34.1%), brotli 132.7 -> 114.8 KB (-13.5%). Adding the gradient bloom strip on top = 49,001 elements (-45.1%), raw 2,728 KB (-38.1%). Site-wide across all 1,867 built pages the non-gradient package is raw HTML 169.9 MB -> 120.7 MB (-29.0%) and 3,285,198 -> 2,575,574 elements (-21.6%); across the 99 pages over 300 KB it is -37.3% raw / -18.8% brotli / -24.3% elements. But 49,001 is still 35x Lighthouse's 1,400-element warning threshold, and the accessibility gatherer that died with PROTOCOL_TIMEOUT on 40+ audits (plants-index.mobile.json) would likely still die. The residual floor is ~29 elements per card x 1,677 cards, and ~17 of those 29 are the four-fact spec block (4 span.spec + 4 svg + 4 use + 4 span.sv + 1 div.specs).
findings.impact: Sets honest expectations for the planning session: the markup package buys ~350 ms of mobile main thread and a 24-45% DOM cut, but /plants/ remains an outlier page that breaks tooling. Anyone expecting the Lighthouse a11y score to come back from null after this work will be disappointed.
findings.suggestedFix: Do not propose pagination or virtualization as part of this package: global.css:196-202 records "every plant is one hop from /plants/" as deliberate SEO design, and the client-side filter at src/pages/plants/index.astro:87 requires every card in the DOM. If the DOM count must actually come down, the honest lever is dropping the 4-fact spec block from cards on the LISTING pages only (17 of 29 residual nodes/card, would take /plants/ to roughly 20k elements) - a design change, and a separate decision. Flag it, do not bundle it.

findings.title: Non-overlapping byte budget for /plants/index.html (static-analysis.json's specBlocks double-counts inlineSvg)
findings.evidence: static-analysis.json's `specBlocks` (1,978,361 B) OVERLAPS `inlineSvg` (1,540,401 B) because the spec span contains the icon svg; naively summing its `parts` gives 5,357,277 B against a 4,516,415 B page (119%). Direct disjoint segmentation of the real dist/plants/index.html, which sums to exactly 4,516,415 B (100.0%): spec icon 365,034 B (8.1%); bloomstrip containers + aria-label 249,139 B (5.5%); div.head + name + botanical 200,104 B (4.4%); the card itself 89,812 B (2.0%); residual card chrome (div.body, closers) 47,204 B (1.0%); EVERYTHING that is not a card (head, nav, filters, 2 JSON-LD blocks, 6 inline scripts, footer) 20,715 B (0.5%). 1,677 cards at 2,681 raw bytes and 53.1 elements each; 99.5% of the page is card markup.
findings.impact: Anchors the plan. The four attackable segments are the icons (34.1%), the bloom cells (16.6%), the data attrs (8.6%) and the months row (8.1%) = 67.4% of the page. Note the per-card cost is essentially identical on every card-grid page (static-analysis.json perCardRawBytes: 2,693 on /plants/, 2,692 full-sun, 2,683 zone/8, 2,722 bloom-combo, 2,743 drought-tolerant), so any per-card fix multiplies across all ~98 heavy pages with no per-page work.
findings.suggestedFix: No fix; use this budget to cost the others. Also note for the record: 14,999 of the 20,124 bloom cells carry no colour at all and are emitted as `` - an empty `style` attribute (BloomStrip.astro:17/:24 `style={c ? ... : ""}`). Emitting no attribute at all instead of an empty one saves 6 bytes x 14,999 = 90 KB raw for free, if the gradient rewrite is NOT taken.

findings.title: 66 community plants have no cardSm rail, so every grid ships a 1280x720 JPEG (~107 KB) into a 217x149 card
findings.evidence: dist/plants/index.html contains 1,611 `cards-sm/*.webp` src + 66 `cards/community/*.jpg` src = 1,677 cards; 66 distinct community files across the whole build, referenced by 3,165 tags (measured by walking dist/). Measured transfer sizes from lighthouse network-requests across all 14 runs: community jpg n=9 distinct, min=81,593 B, median=110,476 B, mean=106,623 B, max=129,071 B; cards-sm webp n=98 distinct, mean=13,536 B. Cause: PlantCard.astro:8 `plant.cardSm?.url || plant.card?.url || plant.image?.url` and HeroTiles.astro:15 fall through to the full card when cardSm is absent. Lighthouse image-delivery-insight, home.mobile: giant-coneflower.jpg totalBytes=101,711, wastedBytes=94,761, subitem "This image file is larger than it needs to be (722x720) for its displayed dimensions (272x153)" = 93,553 B. hub-trait.desktop: 4 community jpgs = 449,289 B, 423,191 B wasted.
findings.impact: 66 plants (3.9% of 1,677) account for ~6.71 MB of the ~27.5 MB of images a full scroll of /plants/ downloads (24% of the image bytes from 4% of the cards). They are the single largest image objects on the home page, every hub, every bloom-calendar combo, and the companion strips. On home/mobile the eager hero tile is one of them: 101,711 B for an 88x88 CSS square.
findings.suggestedFix: Studio-side: publish a `cards-sm/<slug>.webp` 640x360 derivative for the 66 community plants exactly as it does for the other 1,611. No directory code changes needed: the existing fallback chain (PlantCard.astro:8, HeroTiles.astro:15) picks cardSm automatically the moment it exists. Interim directory-only mitigation would require an edge resizer (see no-image-pipeline finding); there is no in-repo fix.

findings.title: Home mobile LCP is a lazy, unprioritised hero tile; the one tile that IS prioritised is the slowest image on the page
findings.evidence: lighthouse/home.mobile.json: perf 82 (worst page), LCP 4,954 ms. lcp-discovery-insight score=0 with checklist priorityHinted=false, eagerlyLoaded=false; the node is `div.hero-visual > div.hero-tiles > a.ht-tile > img`, path ...DIV,1,A,0,IMG (tile index 1), snippet ``, boundingRect 88x88 at top 577. HeroTiles.astro:31-32 sets `loading={i === 0 ? "eager" : "lazy"}` and `fetchpriority={i === 0 ? "high" : undefined}`. dist/index.html has 20 ht-tile images, all rendered at 88x88 (mobile) / 103x103 (desktop) per axe dom.images. Tile 0 is giant-coneflower.jpg, a 101,711 B community JPEG (see community-jpg-no-cardsm), so it paints LAST; LCP only upgrades on a LARGER paint, so the first equal-sized tile to paint (Sea Holly, a 12,250 B webp) wins the LCP.
findings.impact: Every hero tile is the same size, so the LCP element is whichever tile paints first, which is layout- and network-dependent. The index-0 assumption in the code is wrong at any viewport, and it currently hands the single fetchpriority=high hint to the heaviest image on the page. Home/mobile is the worst-scoring page in the whole audit (82) and its LCP (4,954 ms) is 2x the next worst page.
findings.suggestedFix: In HeroTiles.astro:31-32, make the tiles that can be above the fold eager and hinted rather than only index 0: e.g. `loading={i < 6 ? "eager" : "lazy"}` and `fetchpriority={i < 6 ? "high" : undefined}` (6 covers the first two rows at 412px; all 20 tiles are 88-103 px squares, so eager-loading all of them is also defensible: at the measured 13,536 B cardSm mean that is ~270 KB, but only once the community-jpg rail is fixed). Do NOT add a <link rel=preload>: lcp-discovery already reports requestDiscoverable=true, so discovery is not the problem, the loading/priority attributes are.

findings.title: One 640x360 (16:9) rail feeds every card thumb, but the thumb box is 16/11 with object-fit:cover, so 17.8% of every card image is downloaded, decoded, then cropped away
findings.evidence: global.css:204-205 `.card .thumb { aspect-ratio: 16 / 11; }` + `.card .thumb img { object-fit: cover; }`; the rail is 16:9 (640x360). Lighthouse image-delivery-insight computes the post-crop effective source and reports it: "This image file is larger than it needs to be (526x360) for its displayed dimensions (384x216)" (home.mobile, potentilla-abbotswood.webp, 33,016 B, 19,194 B wasted). 526/640 = 82.2%, i.e. 17.8% of the pixels of all 54,077 cardSm tags are never visible. Same 16/11 crop on the detail hero: global.css:279-280, LH plant-detail.mobile "(1052x720) for displayed (805x453)". No card thumb anywhere carries srcset or sizes: static-analysis srcsetPresent=1611, and I confirmed all 1,611 are the plant-detail heroes (1,677 plant pages minus the 66 community ones with no cardSm). Per-page cardSm waste from LH: home.mobile 197,691 B over 20 images; plants-index.desktop 315,901 B over 28; hub-trait.desktop 277,932 B over 26; bloom-combo.desktop 301,845 B over 27.
findings.impact: Every card-grid page (home, /plants/, 6 hub families, every bloom-calendar combo) pays 6.5-19 KB of avoidable bytes per card. A full scroll of /plants/ pulls 1,611 cardSm at a measured 13,536 B mean = ~20.8 MB, of which ~3.7 MB is cropped away unseen. Cards render at 217x149 CSS (desktop) / 180x123 (mobile), so even the visible 526x360 is ~2x the linear size needed at DPR 1 and roughly right only at DPR 2.
findings.suggestedFix: Two independent levers. (a) Directory-only, no new assets: change `.card .thumb` (global.css:204) and `.detail-media` (global.css:279) to `aspect-ratio: 16 / 9` so the box matches the rail and nothing is cropped - this recovers the 17.8% immediately but changes the visual proportions of every card, so it needs a screenshot. (b) Studio-side: publish a card rail whose aspect matches the box (16:11, e.g. 440x302) and/or a second smaller rail (e.g. 360w) so the directory can emit srcset/sizes on PlantCard.astro:64-66. Do not add srcset with the rails that exist today (640 and 1280): there is nothing between them to select.

findings.title: Real-photo gallery thumbnails are 300x400 WebP at 31.7-40.0 KB each (0.31 bytes/px) and are shown at 200x150 with 44% of each one cropped off
findings.evidence: dist/plants/aster/index.html gallery imgs: ``. axe dom.images for plant-detail: naturalW/H = 300x400 for all four (the declared 768x1024 is the FULL photo's size, not the thumb's, per PlantGallery.astro:26 `width={ph.w} height={ph.h}`). Measured transfer (lighthouse plant-detail.mobile network-requests): 32,303 / 38,647 / 37,579 / 40,677 B = 149,206 B of thumbnails vs 31,856 B for the hero. resourceSize mean 36,665 B for a 120,000-pixel image = 0.31 bytes/px. Lighthouse image-delivery-insight names compression, not size, as the dominant cause: "Increasing the image compression factor could improve this image's download size" - 28,824 / 26,786 / 25,714 / 20,446 B wasted (plant-detail.mobile), 101,770 B total. Display: global.css:802-808 `.photo-thumb { width: 200px; aspect-ratio: 4/3 }` + `object-fit: cover` on a 3:4 portrait source, so LH reports the effective source as 300x224 - 176 of the 400 source rows (44%) are cropped. 352 thumbs across 135 gallery pages; histogram 1:36, 2:33, 3:31, 4:23, 5:7, 6:5 pages; the worst (autumn-fern, 6 photos) is ~215 KB of thumbnails at the measured mean.
findings.impact: On the 135 plant pages with a gallery, the thumbnail strip is the heaviest block on the page (4.7x the LCP hero on /plants/aster/). It is correctly below the fold and lazy, so it does not hit LCP, but it costs a scrolling reader 100-220 KB for images that render at 200x150.
findings.suggestedFix: Studio-side: re-encode the `_t.webp` derivative at the aspect it is actually shown in (4:3, e.g. 400x300) and at a normal quality (~q75, ~0.10 bytes/px, roughly 10 KB) instead of 300x400 at 0.31 bytes/px. Directory-side, once that lands, also fix PlantGallery.astro:26 to declare the THUMB's intrinsic size on the thumb (the full photo's w/h is still needed for the lightbox, but it can be carried on a data- attribute; the lightbox script at PlantGallery.astro:80-84 currently reads it off the img's width/height attributes, so both sides must change together).

findings.title: The header/footer logo is a 14,534-byte PNG (1.94 bytes/px) rendered at 34x34 on all 1,866 pages
findings.evidence: public/bloomsee-logo-96.png measured: 14,534 B, 96x78 px = 1.941 bytes/px (a normally-compressed logo PNG is 0.1-0.3). Referenced by 3,732 tags (2 per page x 1,866): Header.astro:10 (width=34 height=34) and Footer.astro:8 (width=30 height=30). Lighthouse image-delivery-insight flags it on EVERY one of the 14 runs: totalBytes 14,534, wastedBytes 14,055 (mobile) / 14,377 (desktop), split "Using a modern image format (WebP, AVIF) or increasing the image compression" = 13,286 B and "larger than it needs to be (96x78) for its displayed dimensions (60x48)" = 8,951 B. Cached 24 h (public/_headers:52-53). Network priority=Low. Secondary: the asset is 96x78 (1.23:1) but the attrs say 34x34 (1:1); global.css:393 `object-fit: contain` letterboxes it, so the reserved box is ~23% taller than the painted mark.
findings.impact: ~13 KB of the 14.5 KB is recoverable on every first page load and every post-24h revisit, on the only image the directory itself puts above the fold on all 1,866 pages. LH says the ideal is ~1.2 KB.
findings.suggestedFix: Ship the mark as a 68x68 WebP (2x the 34px render; ~1-2 KB at the same quality) and point Header.astro:10 / Footer.astro:8 at it, or vectorise the actual rose-in-eye artwork to inline SVG. Note src/components/Mark.astro is an inline SVG but it is a DIFFERENT, simpler eye mark, not the brand logo, so it is not a drop-in swap. AVIF is not needed at this size. Keep the full-size public/bloomsee-logo.png (181 KB): tools/pinterest/cover.ts reads it (Header.astro:8-9).

findings.title: The plant-detail hero srcset does the exact opposite of what its comment claims: phones get the 1280 rail, desktop gets the 640 one
findings.evidence: src/pages/plants/[slug].astro:29-36 comment: "Responsive hero: phones get the 640x360 small card instead of the full 1280x720 (half the LCP bytes on the most numerous page type); desktop at 2x DPR still gets the full card." Measured from lighthouse network-requests: plant-detail.mobile (screenEmulation 412x823, deviceScaleFactor 1.75) fetched `plant-cutouts/cards/aster.webp` at 31,856 B transfer, priority High; plant-detail.desktop (1350x940, DPR 1) fetched `plant-cutouts/cards-sm/aster.webp` at 13,035 B. Cause: `sizes="(max-width: 860px) 100vw, 560px"` ([slug].astro:36) means a 412 CSS px slot; 412 x 1.75 = 721 device px > 640, so Chrome selects the 1280w candidate. Real phones are DPR 2-3, so 412 x 3 = 1,236 - every phone takes the 1280 rail. Desktop needs 560 x 1 = 560, so the 640w rail wins. LH image-delivery on plant-detail.mobile: cards/aster.webp "larger than it needs to be (1052x720) for its displayed dimensions (805x453)", 16,300 B wasted.
findings.impact: The LCP image on the 1,611 non-community plant pages (the most numerous page type) is ~19 KB heavier on mobile than the code believes. Mobile LCP is still 2,329 ms and lcp-discovery passes, so this is a byte problem, not a discovery problem; but the comment is inverted and will mislead the next change.
findings.suggestedFix: Two parts. (a) Directory, now: fix the comment at [slug].astro:29-31 so it states what actually happens. (b) The real fix is a rail between 640w and 1280w: ask the Studio to publish a ~960w card derivative and add it to the srcset at [slug].astro:33-35, which is exactly what a 412 CSS px slot at DPR 2 wants. Changing `sizes` alone would only force phones onto a rail that is too small for their DPR.

findings.title: The 66 community plant pages serve a raw ~107 KB JPEG as their LCP hero with no srcset and no responsive rail at all
findings.evidence: src/pages/plants/[slug].astro:33-35: `heroSrcset` is only defined when `img === plant.card?.url && plant.cardSm?.url` - community plants have no cardSm, so heroSrcset and heroSizes are both undefined and [slug].astro:122 emits a bare ``. This is exactly the srcsetPresent=1611 number in static-analysis.json (1,677 plant pages minus 66 community). The hero is the LCP element on plant-detail (LH lcp-breakdown node `article > div.detail-top > div.detail-media > img`, both form factors). The community card mean is 106,623 B measured (min 81,593, max 129,071).
findings.impact: On 66 of 1,677 plant pages the LCP image is a ~107 KB JPEG with no smaller candidate, versus 13-32 KB on the other 1,611. Compounds community-jpg-no-cardsm; listed separately because here it lands on the LCP.
findings.suggestedFix: Resolved automatically by the Studio publishing cards-sm for community plants (see community-jpg-no-cardsm): [slug].astro:33-35 then produces a srcset for them like every other plant. No directory change needed.

findings.title: All three Supabase image classes are served Cache-Control: no-cache, so a repeat visit pays a conditional GET per image before anything paints
findings.evidence: Live curl -I today, all three classes: `plant-cutouts/cards-sm/aster.webp` -> `Cache-Control: no-cache`, ETag "3e5120eb...", 12,422 B; `plant-cutouts/cards/community/giant-coneflower.jpg` -> `Cache-Control: no-cache`, 101,711 B; `plant-photos/aster/<hash>_t.webp` -> `Cache-Control: no-cache`, 31,668 B. Measured revalidation cost (3 conditional GETs with If-None-Match, warm connection): status=304, size_download=0, total 0.277 s / 0.133 s / 0.120 s, time_connect 0.022 s. /plants/ mobile first paint makes 10 Supabase image requests (LH network-requests, 280,614 B of images total); a full scroll would make 1,677. By contrast the directory's own assets are correct: public/_headers:36-69 sets immutable on /_astro/* and /fonts/*, 24 h on the logo/icons/previews.
findings.impact: Repeat-visit BYTES are ~0 (the 304s return nothing), but every image still costs a round trip before it can paint, and the LCP image is one of them: on /plants/ that is 10 blocking conditional GETs on the initial viewport, 1,677 across a full scroll. Also means the browser can never paint a cached card offline or on a flaky link.
findings.suggestedFix: Studio-side, unchanged from the existing PERF-1 entry: upload the plant-cutouts and plant-photos objects with a `cacheControl` value (Supabase Storage defaults to no-cache when the upload does not set one). The image URLs already carry a `?v=<ts>` buster on the community cards, so a long max-age is safe there. Note for whoever quotes numbers: Lighthouse's cache-insight reports cacheLifetimeMs=3600000 for the community JPEGs, which contradicts the live no-cache header; trust the curl.

findings.title: /previews/cool-border.jpg is a 127 KB 1280x776 JPEG shown at 660x400; 71% of it is waste, and one is planned per guide
findings.evidence: public/previews/cool-border.jpg measured: 127,141 B, 1280x776, 0.128 bytes/px. Rendered by src/pages/guides/[slug].astro:138 `` (note: the declared 720 does not match the file's 776, though global.css:542 forces aspect-ratio 16/9 with object-fit:cover so nothing shifts). Lighthouse image-delivery-insight guide.mobile: totalBytes 127,141, wastedBytes 90,803, "larger than it needs to be (1280x722) for its displayed dimensions (660x400)"; guide.desktop wastedBytes 73,873. It is the 4th-largest non-HTML asset in dist (static-analysis nonHtmlAssets). CLAUDE.md says the garden-preview card is authored one guide at a time ("next the hot border"), so this cost multiplies by guide.
findings.impact: 90.8 KB of avoidable bytes on the guide page today, and the same on every future guide that gets a preview card. It is lazy-loaded and below the intro, so it does not touch LCP (guide mobile LCP is the guide hero, 2,703 ms, lcp-discovery passes).
findings.suggestedFix: Re-export the hand-composed preview as WebP at the size it is actually shown (a 1280w WebP at q80 lands around 25-40 KB versus 127 KB), and add a second smaller rail plus srcset/sizes on guides/[slug].astro:138 if the mobile 660px display is worth a separate file. Also correct the hardcoded height="720" to the real 776, or crop the source to a true 16:9 so the attribute is honest. Fix the export step before the hot-border preview ships, so the pattern does not propagate.

findings.title: Astro's image pipeline is not used at all: no <Image>, no image config, zero image assets in _astro - so the directory has no lever on any image it serves
findings.evidence: grep for `astro:assets|<Image|<Picture|getImage` across src/ returns nothing. astro.config.mjs has no `image` block (site, trailingSlash, build.format, sitemap only). dist/_astro/ contains exactly one file: Layout.BDtOC_yW.css. Every image on the site is either a remote Supabase URL (57,626 of 61,631 img tags) or a public/ file copied verbatim (3,733 relative + 2 pinimg). dist currently holds 1,889 files.
findings.impact: Defensible for the 57,626 remote catalog images: they are the Studio's product, and pulling 1,677+ originals through sharp at build time on Cloudflare Pages would be a large, fragile build. But it is also why every finding above is either "ask the Studio" or "do nothing": the directory cannot re-rail, re-crop, re-format, or re-cache a single catalog image. It is a straight miss for the ~10 LOCAL public/ images, which are shipped completely unoptimised (the logo at 1.94 bytes/px, cool-border.jpg at 127 KB, icon-512.png at 205 KB).
findings.suggestedFix: Do not adopt astro:assets for the remote catalog: the fixes belong upstream. Two things are worth doing. (1) Optimise the ~10 local public/ images by hand or with a one-off script (see logo-png-14kb-every-page and cool-border-preview-jpg): they are static, they change rarely, and no pipeline is needed. (2) If the Studio cannot own the rails/cache, evaluate Cloudflare Image Resizing (/cdn-cgi/image/<opts>/<supabase-url>) in front of the catalog: it would fix rails, format, crop, AND the no-cache header in one place, owned by the directory's Cloudflare config. I could not verify whether the current Pages plan includes it: UNVERIFIED. Headroom exists either way: at 1,889 files today, ~3 derivative rails x 1,677 plants (5,031 files) would still fit under Cloudflare Pages' per-deployment file cap (20,000, recalled from memory, UNVERIFIED).

findings.title: AVIF is a rounding error here: Lighthouse's own split says right-sizing beats re-formatting 6x on the only non-WebP images
findings.evidence: static-analysis byExtension: webp 54,461, png 3,732, jpg 3,167, gif 1. The catalog is ALREADY WebP. The 3,167 jpgs decompose exactly: 3,165 community card tags (66 files), 1 /previews/cool-border.jpg, 1 i.pinimg.com thumbnail in the Pinterest verification page. The 3,732 pngs are all /bloomsee-logo-96.png (2 per page x 1,866). On the worst JPEG (giant-coneflower, home.mobile) Lighthouse splits the 94,761 B of waste as 15,065 B from "modern image format (WebP, AVIF) or increasing compression" versus 93,553 B from "larger than it needs to be": sizing is 6.2x the format lever. On cards-sm (already WebP) LH lists NO format subitem at all, only sizing. The one place format dominates is the logo: 13,286 B of its 14,055 B waste is format/compression.
findings.impact: None on its own. Recorded so a planning session does not spend effort on an AVIF pipeline: the wins are (in order) the missing cardSm rail for community plants, the hero-tile eager/priority bug, the 16/11 crop, the gallery thumb encode, and the logo. AVIF would add a second rail per image, a Safari-version tax, and a decode cost, for a few percent.
findings.suggestedFix: Skip AVIF. Compress the logo to WebP (one file), re-encode cool-border.jpg to WebP (one file), and get cards-sm WebP derivatives for the 66 community plants from the Studio. That captures effectively all of the recoverable format bytes.

findings.title: Three components declare width/height from a DIFFERENT image object than the one they put in src, which is a latent CLS bug
findings.evidence: PlantCard.astro:64-66 sets `src={img}` where img = `plant.cardSm?.url || plant.card?.url || plant.image?.url` (line 8) but `width={plant.cardSm?.w ?? 640} height={plant.cardSm?.h ?? 360}` - so for the 66 community plants it serves a 1280x720 file while declaring 640x360. HeroTiles.astro:26-30 does the same (`p.cardSm?.url || p.card?.url` with `width={p.cardSm?.w ?? 640}`); confirmed in dist/index.html: `` for a 1280x720 file. PlantCompanions.astro:34 hardcodes `width="160" height="90"` regardless of which of `cp.cardSm || cp.card || cp.image` resolved. Harmless today only because every catalog rail happens to be 16:9, so the declared aspect ratio is right even when the declared size is wrong.
findings.impact: Zero CLS today (LH unsized-images scores 1 on all 14 runs; CLS 0.000 everywhere except plants-index mobile at 0.003). But the moment the Studio publishes any rail at a different aspect (e.g. the 16:11 card rail proposed above, or a square community crop), every card, hero tile, and companion image on the site gets a layout shift, and the bug will be invisible in review because the code reads as if the numbers came from the served file.
findings.suggestedFix: Resolve the image object once and declare from it, as PlantPick.astro:28-29 (`width={src?.w ?? 640} height={src?.h ?? 360}` against the same `src` it renders) and guides/index.astro:58 (`width={src.w} height={src.h}`) already do correctly. In PlantCard.astro replace line 8's url-only pick with an object pick (`const src = plant.cardSm ?? plant.card ?? plant.image`) and use `src.url / src.w / src.h`; same shape in HeroTiles.astro:15 and PlantCompanions.astro:34.

findings.title: The Pinterest verification page is the only page in the build with external images and image preloads, and the CSP blocks all of them
findings.evidence: dist/pinterest-144d4.html (verbatim from Pinterest) is the ONLY source of the 2 non-Supabase, non-relative img tags in static-analysis byHost (s.pinimg.com 1, i.pinimg.com 1) and the ONLY `<link rel="preload" as="image">` in the entire build (2 of them, both to pinimg.com; grep over dist for `as="image"` returns 2 hits, both in this file). public/_headers:32 sets `img-src 'self' data: https://*.supabase.co`, so both images, both preloads, and the page's s.pinimg.com background are blocked; it also carries an inline <script> with a Pinterest nonce that `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://static.cloudflareinsights.com` will not run.
findings.impact: Cosmetic only. Pinterest verifies the domain via the meta p:domain_verify tag / file presence, which still works, and CLAUDE.md says to leave the file in place. Worth knowing so nobody chases the CSP console errors on that URL or counts its 2 preloads as the site's image preloads.
findings.suggestedFix: Leave it (CLAUDE.md explicitly says to). If the console noise ever matters, the file can be reduced to just its <head> meta tag, which is the only part Pinterest reads. Do not add pinimg.com to the CSP for it.

findings.title: Half the plant library (834/1677) has a bare cultivar epithet as its H1, its JSON-LD name, and its title prefix
findings.evidence:
dist/plants/agapanthus-midnight-blue/index.html:

Midnight Blue

, Midnight Blue (Agapanthus 'Midnight Blue') · Bloom Time, Zones & Care | BloomsEye, and JSON-LD "@type":"Thing","name":"Midnight Blue". Source: src/pages/plants/[slug].astro:125 (

{plant.commonName}

) and src/lib/seo.ts:100 (name: p.commonName). Measured over the built output: 834 of 1677 plant pages have an h1 that is exactly the quoted epithet inside their own botanical name (Adirondack / Malus 'Adirondack'; Peter Pan / Agapanthus 'Peter Pan'; Gladiator / Allium 'Gladiator'; Purity / Cosmos bipinnatus 'Purity'). Live catalog confirms: commonName="Midnight Blue", groupName="Agapanthus". The /plants/ ItemList JSON-LD carries the same bare names (e.g. "Adirondack").
findings.impact: Half the site's pages give Google and AI answer engines no entity name to attach the page to. "Peter Pan" and "Gladiator" match no gardening query and no plant entity. seo.ts:76 states the plantLd exists "for comprehension + AI answer engines", but the primary `name` it hands them is a meaningless string on 50% of the library. Also drives the 24 duplicate-H1 collisions (e.g. "Snowflake" on /plants/catmint-snowflake/, /plants/creeping-phlox-snowflake/, /plants/snowflake/ - three unrelated plants).
findings.suggestedFix: Compose a display name once and use it for the h1, the title, the Thing.name and the card label. `groupName` is already in the Plant type (src/lib/types.ts:44), already normalized at build (src/data/loadPlants.ts:113), and populated on 1511/1677 live records, but it is read NOWHERE in src/ (grep confirms: only the type + the loader + sample-plants.json). It composes a real name for 830 of the 834. Two guards are needed and both are checked: (a) 137 records have commonName === groupName (the group defaults, e.g. slug `agapanthus`), so only prepend when the commonName does not already contain the groupName, or the h1 becomes "Agapanthus Agapanthus"; (b) 4 bare records have no groupName (sally-holmes, scarlet-fire, stanwell-perpetual, annabelle) and would need the genus taken from botanicalName. Also note some groupNames read awkwardly when prepended ("Dahlias", "English (David Austin) Roses"), so the composition needs a look at the distinct groupName list. The catalog already does this correctly for a few records ("Agastache 'Apricot Sprite'", "Agastache 'Firebird'" appear as commonNames), so the fix could alternatively be pushed upstream to the Studio.

findings.title: Cloudflare injects an AI-crawler block into the live robots.txt that the repo has no record of, and the repo's own file claims the opposite
findings.evidence: Fetched live https://bloomseye.com/robots.txt: a "BEGIN Cloudflare Managed content" block is PREPENDED above the repo's file. It sets `User-agent: * / Content-Signal: search=yes,ai-train=no,use=reference / Allow: /` and then `Disallow: /` for Amazonbot, Applebot-Extended, Bytespider, CCBot, ClaudeBot, CloudflareBrowserRenderingCrawler, Google-Extended, GPTBot, meta-externalagent. The repo's public/robots.txt is 5 lines and line 1 reads "# BloomsEye, the whole library is public and crawlable." That comment is now false for 9 named agents. src/lib/seo.ts:76 comments that plantLd exists "for comprehension + AI answer engines".
findings.impact: Honest read, not an alarm: Googlebot, Bingbot, and the answer-engine FETCHERS (OAI-SearchBot, ChatGPT-User, Claude-SearchBot, Claude-User, PerplexityBot) are NOT in the block list, so search indexing and live AI retrieval still work. What is actually lost is training-corpus and grounding presence: Common Crawl (CCBot, an input to many downstream tools), GPTBot, ClaudeBot, Google-Extended, Meta AI, Apple Intelligence, Amazon. Note this is arguably INTENTIONAL and consistent with the site's own Terms (src/pages/terms.astro explicitly forbids using the content "to build, train, or improve a machine-learning model, an AI system, or a competing dataset"). The defect is not the policy, it is that the policy is invisible in the repo: anyone editing public/robots.txt cannot see the managed block, cannot remove it by editing that file, and is actively misled by its comment.
findings.suggestedFix: Two parts. (1) Decide and record the policy: if ai-train=no is intended (it matches terms.astro), say so in public/robots.txt's comment and in CLAUDE.md, and fix the false "the whole library is public and crawlable" line. If it is NOT intended, it must be turned off in the Cloudflare dashboard (AI Crawl Control / managed robots.txt) - editing public/robots.txt cannot remove it, since Cloudflare merges at the edge. (2) Separately reconsider ClaudeBot and CCBot specifically, which are the two most likely to cost real AI-surface visibility. Do NOT apply anything here without an explicit decision from the owner: this is a business/licensing call, not a bug.

findings.title: itemListLd declares the full count but lists only the first 60 items, on 111 pages
findings.evidence: src/lib/seo.ts:161-162: `numberOfItems: plants.length` but `itemListElement: plants.slice(0, 60)`. Verified in the built output: 111 pages have numberOfItems != itemListElement.length. dist/plants/index.html declares numberOfItems: 1677, lists 60. Worst offenders: /plants/zone/8/ (1217 vs 60), /plants/light/full-sun/ (1273 vs 60), /bloom-calendar/zone-8/july/ (617 vs 60), /plants/blooming-in/july/ (966 vs 60). Because loadPlants sorts by commonName, the 60 listed on /plants/ are an alphabetical prefix (first: "Abbotswood", 60th: "Apricot Sweet Alyssum") - not a curated top-N, just everything up to "Ap". The companions ItemList on plant pages is unaffected (8 declared, 8 listed).
findings.impact: The structured data asserts something the markup does not contain. Google's ItemList/carousel guidance expects the listed items to BE the list; a consumer that trusts numberOfItems gets a list 4% the size it was promised. Crawl-discovery impact is near zero (every plant is in the visible HTML and in the sitemap), so this is a correctness/trust issue rather than a traffic one.
findings.suggestedFix: Either set numberOfItems to the number actually emitted (`const items = plants.slice(0, 60); ... numberOfItems: items.length`), which is a one-line change in seo.ts:156-169, or raise/remove the cap for the small hubs. Do not just raise it for /plants/: 1677 ListItems would add materially to a page that is already 4.4 MB raw and 89k DOM nodes.

findings.title: 1,605 of 1,677 plant titles exceed 60 characters (1,309 exceed 70; max 127), so the differentiating suffix is truncated in SERPs
findings.evidence: src/pages/plants/[slug].astro:70 builds `${commonName} (${botanicalName}) · Bloom Time, Zones & Care`, then Layout.astro:36 appends ` | BloomsEye`. Measured across the built output: plant <title> min 50 / median 78 / max 127 chars. Longest: dist/plants/new-england-aster-harringtons-pink/index.html at 127 chars ("New England Aster 'Harrington's Pink' (Symphyotrichum novae-angliae 'Harrington's Pink') · Bloom Time, Zones & Care | BloomsEye"). 1,730 of all 1,867 pages exceed 60 chars.
findings.impact: Google truncates titles around 580px (roughly 60 chars). The 38-char boilerplate "· Bloom Time, Zones & Care | BloomsEye" plus the brand is cut off on the majority of plant pages, so the keywords the suffix was added to win ("bloom time", "zones", "care") are never displayed. Cultivar titles are also self-redundant: "Midnight Blue (Agapanthus 'Midnight Blue')" says the epithet twice.
findings.suggestedFix: Fix this together with `bare-epithet-names`, since both come from the same commonName/botanicalName composition at [slug].astro:70. Once the h1/name is a composed display name (e.g. "Agapanthus 'Midnight Blue'"), the title no longer needs the botanical name in parens, which removes the redundancy and most of the length. Consider shortening the suffix (e.g. "· Bloom Time & Care") and clamping the whole title.

findings.title: Duplicate cultivar slugs quantified: exactly 3 pairs / 6 pages, identical <title> and <h1>, with CONFLICTING facts
findings.evidence: Scanned all 1,677 built plant pages for same-h1 + same-botanical-name. Exactly 3 groups, 6 pages, all Anemone blanda: /plants/anemone-blanda-blue/ vs /plants/windflower-blue-shades/ (both <title>Blue Shades (Anemone blanda 'Blue Shades') · Bloom Time, Zones & Care | BloomsEye</title>); same for Pink Star and White Splendour. The facts DISAGREE: anemone-blanda-* say "Zones 5-9 | Part shade | 0.15 m × 0.12 m", windflower-* say "Zones 4-8 | Part sun | 0.15 m × 0.10 m". These are the only 3 duplicate <title> groups on the entire site. Live catalog confirms both slugs carry botanicalName "Anemone blanda 'Blue Shades'" with different `group` values (anemone vs windflower).
findings.impact: Six pages, 0.36% of the library. Two URLs compete for the same query with contradictory hardiness and light data, which is worse than the duplication itself: a visitor or an answer engine gets zone 5-9 or zone 4-8 depending on which page it lands on. Smaller in scope than CLAUDE.md's phrasing implies, but the data conflict is a real quality problem.
findings.suggestedFix: Directory-side stopgap: three 301 lines in public/_redirects pointing the windflower-* slugs at the anemone-blanda-* ones (or vice versa; pick whichever the catalog treats as canonical). But note a 301 does not fix the conflicting data, only the duplication, and Cloudflare Pages runs redirects BEFORE static assets, so the redirected page stops being served entirely. The durable fix is Studio-side dedupe of the two group records.

findings.title: Plant pages emit 3 to 4 disconnected top-level JSON-LD blocks with no @id and no WebPage node
findings.evidence: dist/plants/coneflower/index.html emits 3 separate <script type="application/ld+json"> blocks: BreadcrumbList, Thing, and an ItemList named "What pairs well with Purple Coneflower". None carries an @id. Pages with question-shaped intel add a 4th (FAQPage, 61 pages). Verified across the site: 4,093 ld+json blocks total, zero with @id. src/components/Layout.astro:105-107 just maps each object to its own script tag.
findings.impact: A consumer sees four unrelated islands. Nothing says the FAQPage's questions are ABOUT the Thing, or that the companions ItemList belongs to it, or which node is the page's mainEntity. This is exactly the comprehension the seo.ts:76 comment is aiming for, and it is the cheapest structured-data win available.
findings.suggestedFix: Wrap the per-page nodes in a single @graph with stable @ids: a WebPage node (@id: <url>#webpage) with `breadcrumb`, `mainEntity` pointing at the Thing (@id: <url>#plant), and the FAQPage / companions ItemList referencing the plant via `about` or `mainEntityOfPage`. All core schema.org, zero rich-result risk. It is a change to seo.ts + Layout.astro (Layout currently assumes an array of independent objects, so the jsonLd prop shape would change).

findings.title: /pinterest-144d4 serves 200 with an empty <title>, no canonical, no noindex, and CSP-blocked third-party images
findings.evidence: dist/pinterest-144d4.html: `<title></title>`, no meta description, no canonical, no meta robots. Live check: https://bloomseye.com/pinterest-144d4.html 308-redirects to /pinterest-144d4, which returns 200 text/html with NO X-Robots-Tag (confirmed via curl -I). It is correctly absent from the sitemap (1,865 URLs = 1,867 built HTML files minus 404.html and pinterest-144d4.html). The page body is Pinterest's own verification markup: "©2019 Pinterest, Inc.", four dofollow links to pinterest.com, and three s.pinimg.com / i.pinimg.com images that the site's own CSP (public/_headers:32, `img-src 'self' data: https://*.supabase.co`) blocks. Cloudflare also promotes its <link rel=preload> tags to an early-hints response header (`Link: <https://s.pinimg.com/images/email/logo.gif>; rel="preload"; as=image`) that the CSP then blocks.
findings.impact: A crawlable, indexable, zero-title, thin-content page on the apex domain carrying a third party's copyright notice and four dofollow outbound links. Low traffic risk (nothing links to it), but it is exactly the kind of page that shows up in Search Console as an indexed page with no title. It also renders as a broken shell for anyone who reaches it, because its own images are CSP-blocked.
findings.suggestedFix: Add an X-Robots-Tag rule to public/_headers. Pinterest's verifier reads the `p:domain_verify` meta from the response body and is not affected by a noindex response header, so this does not break the domain claim. Two lines beside the existing pages.dev rule at public/_headers:23-24, e.g. a `/pinterest-144d4*` path block with `X-Robots-Tag: noindex`. Note that the served URL is the extensionless /pinterest-144d4 (Cloudflare Pages strips .html), so the rule must cover both. CLAUDE.md says leave the file in place, so do not delete it.

findings.title: CLAUDE.md's "No description field in the live catalog" known issue is factually wrong on both halves
findings.evidence: CLAUDE.md Known issues: "No `description` field in the live catalog, so every plant meta description falls back to the generated template". Fetched the live catalog (1,677 records): `description` IS one of the 22 keys, and 66 records have it populated. Confirmed independently in the built output: 66 of 1,677 plant pages have a non-templated meta description (e.g. dist/plants/agastache-apricot-sprite/index.html: "A compact agastache with peachy-apricot flowers and aromatic foliage. Excellent for containers and small garden spaces while maintaining the…", which is the clamp(155) at [slug].astro:64-73 doing its job), and the plantLd Thing.description splits the same way (66 real / 1,611 templated).
findings.impact: An agent reading CLAUDE.md first (which the file instructs) will believe the field does not exist and may propose adding it, or will not realise that 96.1% (1,611/1,677) rather than 100% of descriptions are templated. The real issue is a data-coverage gap in the Studio's catalog, not a missing field.
findings.suggestedFix: Restate the Known issue as: "96% of live catalog records (1,611/1,677) have a null `description`, so their meta description and Thing.description fall back to the generated template (src/pages/plants/[slug].astro:71-73, src/lib/seo.ts:102). 66 records have a real description and use it. Populating the field is Studio-owned." Do not touch the code: loadPlants.ts:105 and the fallbacks are all correct.

findings.title: Every guide meta description (32/32) and 87 of 97 bloom-calendar combos exceed 160 characters
findings.evidence: Measured across the built output. Guides: min 168 / median 193 / max 229 chars. Longest: dist/guides/plants-for-slopes/index.html at 229. bloom-combo: min 158 / median 164 / max 173, 87 of 97 over 160. Also over: /guides/ (206), /bloom-calendar/ (187), / (165), /plants/ (165). Plant pages are all fine (max 155, thanks to clamp(155) at [slug].astro:72). Descriptions come from src/data/guides.ts (`description`) and the template at src/pages/bloom-calendar/[zoneslug]/[month].astro.
findings.impact: Google truncates around 155 to 160 chars, so the back third of every guide description is cut. Low real cost: Google rewrites descriptions from page content most of the time anyway, and these are well-written first 155 chars. Worth a pass, not worth a sprint.
findings.suggestedFix: Trim the 32 `description` strings in src/data/guides.ts to <=155 chars (they are hand-authored, so this is copy editing, not code), and shorten the bloom-combo description template so the longest combo lands under 160. Verify by re-measuring the built output, not by counting the template.

findings.title: Eight guide pages link to a 302, and one of them redirects back to itself
findings.evidence: public/_redirects:12 sends /plants/traits/low-maintenance/ to /guides/low-maintenance-perennials/ (302; confirmed live via curl). Scanning every internal href in the 1,867 built pages: 8 guide pages link to that redirected URL. One of them is /guides/low-maintenance-perennials/ ITSELF, twice: src/data/guides.ts:507 (body copy: "browse the full lists of low-maintenance plants") and src/data/guides.ts:540 (a chip). Other linkers: guides.ts:836, 1369, 1397, 1655, 1723, 1880.
findings.impact: A reader on the low-maintenance-perennials guide who clicks "low-maintenance plants" is redirected straight back to the page they are already on. The other 7 guides pass link equity through a 302 rather than to a real page. Small, but it is the kind of thing a crawl audit surfaces as a self-referential redirect.
findings.suggestedFix: The hub genuinely cannot be built (0 plants carry the low-maintenance tag; tagFacets needs 3+), so the redirect must stay. Instead, change the 8 hrefs in src/data/guides.ts. On /guides/low-maintenance-perennials/ specifically (lines 507 and 540), remove the self-link or point it at a hub that exists (e.g. /plants/traits/drought-tolerant/, which now builds with 377 plants). On the other 6 guides, point the chip directly at /guides/low-maintenance-perennials/ so the link resolves in one hop. The durable fix is Studio-side tagging, at which point the hub builds and the redirect must be deleted (public/_redirects:9-11 already warns about this).

findings.title: plantLd's PropertyValue pairs carry unparseable prose values: no unitCode, no numeric value, no propertyID
findings.evidence: src/lib/seo.ts:78-96. dist/plants/coneflower/index.html emits {"@type":"PropertyValue","name":"Mature height","value":"0.9 m"}, {"name":"USDA hardiness zones","value":"3–9"}, {"name":"Bloom time","value":"Jul–Sep"}. Every value is a display string. The intel properties additionally inline the citation into the value ("...aster yellows. (per University of Illinois and Clemson)", seo.ts:91-95), which the code comment acknowledges is a workaround for PropertyValue having no citation field.
findings.impact: A machine consumer cannot extract "0.9" and "metres" from "0.9 m", or a zone range from an en-dash string. This directly limits the "comprehension" the block exists for (seo.ts:76). Low severity because no rich result depends on it and the human-readable form is still parseable by an LLM.
findings.suggestedFix: For the two numeric properties, add the structured form alongside: `{"@type":"PropertyValue","name":"Mature height","value":0.9,"unitCode":"MTR","unitText":"m"}` (p.matureHeight and p.matureSpread are already numbers at seo.ts:84-85, so the raw value is in hand). Leave the prose properties alone. Fold this in when doing the @graph work rather than as a standalone change.

findings.title: Assessment: Thing IS the right @type for plant pages. Do not switch to Product; Taxon is not worth it
findings.evidence: src/lib/seo.ts:99 emits "@type":"Thing" with name/alternateName/description/url/image/additionalProperty. Verified in dist/plants/coneflower/index.html. Weighing the alternatives against the actual pages: (a) Product would make the pages eligible for product rich results, but the pages carry no offer, price, availability, or review. The only commerce surface is an Amazon SEARCH link (src/lib/site.ts:128-132, and it is currently OFF: AMAZON_ASSOC_TAG is empty, so 0 of the 1,677 built pages render it). Emitting Product with no Offer is a Search Console warning generator and, more importantly, misrepresents a reference page as a purchasable item. Reject. (b) schema.org `Taxon` exists but is in the PENDING section (from the Bioschemas collaboration), not core. It would apply to at most the ~843 species pages that carry a real binomial, not the 834 cultivar pages, and Google ignores it. Marginal. (c) There is no Google rich result for plants at all, which the seo.ts:76 comment already states correctly.
findings.impact: None today. Recording this so a planning session does not burn a cycle re-litigating the type, or worse, "upgrading" to Product and inviting a structured-data penalty.
findings.suggestedFix: Keep Thing. The real structured-data gaps on these pages are, in priority order: (1) the `name` is a bare cultivar epithet on 834 pages (see bare-epithet-names), (2) no @graph wiring or @id (see jsonld-no-graph-wiring), (3) no units on PropertyValue (see propertyvalue-no-units). Fix those three and the plant pages become substantially more machine-comprehensible without changing the type at all. If Taxon is ever added, add it as a SECOND type on species pages only (`"@type": ["Thing", "Taxon"]`), never as a replacement.

findings.title: og:image is a .webp on 1,643 of 1,867 pages; social-preview rendering is unverified
findings.evidence: Counted og:image extensions across the built output: .webp 1,643, .jpg 66 (the community cards), .png 157 (the /og-default.png fallback). Example: dist/plants/agapanthus-midnight-blue/index.html sets og:image to https://nlgxfcklwiwrmkwfvztf.supabase.co/.../cards/agapanthus-midnight-blue.webp at 1280x720. Guides do the same (dist/guides/cool-border/index.html -> cards/delphinium.webp). twitter:image is the same URL.
findings.impact: UNVERIFIED. X/Twitter documents WebP as a supported card format, and Meta's current og:image docs list image/webp. LinkedIn has historically not rendered WebP link previews. I could not verify any of this from the artifacts, and I did not run a Sharing Debugger or Post Inspector. If a consumer rejects WebP, the share preview falls back to no image on 1,643 pages, which would matter for the Pinterest funnel the repo is actively building.
findings.suggestedFix: Do NOT change anything on this finding until it is verified. Run the three URLs through Facebook's Sharing Debugger, LinkedIn's Post Inspector, and a Pinterest pin preview. If a consumer rejects WebP, the cheap hedge is a JPG/PNG og rail published alongside the .webp card (Studio-owned, since the directory only re-points at whatever rails the catalog exposes; loadPlants.ts:44-50 accepts any {url,w,h} on the catalog bucket). Falling back to /og-default.png would be a quality downgrade and is not recommended.

findings.title: 110+ hub and combo titles embed a live catalog count, so they are rewritten on every catalog refresh
findings.evidence: dist/plants/type/rose/index.html: <title>Roses (87) · Browse Roses | BloomsEye</title>. dist/plants/zone/5/index.html: <title>Plants for USDA Zone 5 (1070) · Hardy Garden Plants | BloomsEye</title>. dist/bloom-calendar/zone-6/june/index.html: <title>Zone 6 Plants That Bloom in June (541) · Bloom Calendar | BloomsEye</title>. Meta descriptions embed the same counts. This affects the 52 facet hubs + 97 bloom-calendar combos + /plants/ and /bloom-calendar/.
findings.impact: Every catalog publish that shifts a facet count by one rewrites that page's <title> and description. Google notices title instability and may start rewriting the title itself. Very low severity: the counts are also a genuine quality/freshness signal in the SERP snippet, so this is a real tradeoff, not a clear defect.
findings.suggestedFix: No action recommended without a decision. If title churn is ever observed in Search Console, move the count out of the <title> and keep it in the description and the visible h1/lead, where churn costs nothing. Flagging it so a planning session sees the tradeoff rather than discovering it later.

findings.title: Guides emit og:type=article but no article:published_time / article:modified_time
findings.evidence: dist/guides/cool-border/index.html has <meta property="og:type" content="article"> (from Layout.astro:53 via the type="article" prop at guides/[slug].astro:65) but grepping for `property="article:` returns nothing. The dates DO exist: the Article JSON-LD carries datePublished and dateModified (src/lib/seo.ts:186-187, sourced from guide.datePublished / guide.dateModified), and the page shows "Updated <date>" in the byline (guides/[slug].astro:76).
findings.impact: Essentially none for Google, which reads the JSON-LD, not the OG article tags. The OG article:* tags matter mainly to social scrapers and some feed readers. Noting it only so the omission is a known choice rather than an oversight.
findings.suggestedFix: If wanted: add optional publishedTime/modifiedTime props to Layout.astro and emit `article:published_time` / `article:modified_time` when type==="article". Two lines. Low value; do not prioritise it.

findings.title: robots.txt hardcodes the sitemap origin, ignoring SITE_URL
findings.evidence: public/robots.txt:5 is a literal `Sitemap: https://bloomseye.com/sitemap-index.xml`, while astro.config.mjs:5 reads `process.env.SITE_URL || "https://bloomseye.com"` and src/lib/site.ts:11 honours the same override. Confirmed the live file still carries that exact line.
findings.impact: Zero today (SITE_URL is not overridden). It goes stale silently the day the origin is repointed, and robots.txt is the one file that would keep advertising the old sitemap.
findings.suggestedFix: public/ is copied verbatim by Astro, so a static file cannot interpolate SITE_URL. Move robots.txt to a src/pages/robots.txt.ts endpoint that reads SITE.url from src/lib/site.ts. But be careful: this changes NOTHING about the Cloudflare-injected managed block (see robots-ai-block-undocumented), which is merged at the edge regardless of what this repo serves. Low priority; the two robots findings should be planned together.

findings.title: The fancy-select's keyboard cursor (active option) is invisible: 1.08:1 in light, 1.09:1 in dark
findings.evidence: src/styles/global.css:719 `.fs-opt.fs-active { background: var(--bs-surface-3); }` is the ONLY visual indicator of the aria-activedescendant cursor (src/pages/plants/index.astro:254-268 setActive() adds nothing else). The popup's own background is --bs-surface-2 (global.css:699). Computed contrast of the highlight against the popup: light #E9EEE2 on #F3F6EE = 1.08:1; dark #222A23 on #1C231D = 1.09:1. WCAG needs 3:1 for a state/focus indicator (1.4.11) and a visible focus indicator (2.4.7). axe cannot catch this: it only checks TEXT contrast, and it in fact used #E9EEE2 as the *background* for its one color-contrast violation without ever questioning that the background itself is invisible.
findings.impact: A sighted keyboard user pressing ArrowDown through the 13-month / 12-colour / 10-zone filter listboxes sees essentially nothing move. The DOM focus is on the <ul> (which has no ring of its own), so there is zero on-screen feedback about where they are. Mouse users get the same near-invisible hover highlight (pop mousemove -> setActive, index.astro:343-348). This is the site's primary browse UI.
findings.suggestedFix: Give .fs-opt.fs-active a real indicator, not a background tint: `.fs-opt.fs-active { background: var(--bs-accent-weak); box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 2px var(--bs-accent); }`. --bs-accent measures 3.74:1 against the light surfaces and 6.31:1 against the dark ones, so the inset ring clears 1.4.11 in both themes; --bs-accent-weak alone would not (it is another near-invisible tint).

findings.title: In-prose links carry no underline site-wide: axe found 2 nodes, the real footprint is ~140 pages
findings.evidence: global.css:101-102 sets `a { text-decoration: none }` with underline only on :hover. Only guide prose is exempted (global.css:571-573 `.guide-body p a, .guide-body li a, .qa a, .callout a { text-decoration: underline }`). axe link-in-text-block (serious, wcag2a/wcag141) fired on 2 distinct links x 2 themes: (a) `.photo-gallery-note > a[href="/credits/"]` (PlantGallery.astro:20), link #6fcf8e vs surrounding #a1b09b = 1.19:1 dark and 1.07:1 light (needs 3:1); (b) the /credits/ mailto, 1.62:1 dark / 2.83:1 light. Beyond axe's 12-page sample, the same unstyled-prose-link pattern is on 135 built plant pages (grep -rl photo-gallery-note dist/plants/ = 135), on /privacy/ (4 mailto links in prose), on /terms/ (2), and on the hidden #f-empty "No plants match those filters. Reset" (plants/index.astro:59). All three legal/credits pages wrap their prose in `.article` (privacy.astro:10, credits.astro:7), which has no link rule.
findings.impact: WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color, level A. A colour-blind or low-vision reader cannot tell these are links: the only cue is a hue that carries 1.07:1 against the text around it. It affects the CC BY attribution route ("How photos are sourced") on every gallery page and the support email on the legal pages.
findings.suggestedFix: Extend the rule that already exists at global.css:571-573 rather than adding a new one: `.guide-body p a, .guide-body li a, .qa a, .callout a, .article p a, .article li a, .photo-gallery-note a, #f-empty a { text-decoration: underline; text-underline-offset: 2px; ... }`. Underlining satisfies the rule on its own; no token change needed.

findings.title: Home hero tiles: role=listitem on <a href> is invalid, and the "+N more" tile breaks the list outright
findings.evidence: HeroTiles.astro:13 `<div class="hero-tiles" role="list">`, :20 `role="listitem"` on each `<a class="ht-tile" href=...>`, but :39 the final `<a class="ht-tile ht-more">` has NO role. axe: aria-allowed-role (minor, best-practice, 38 nodes across both themes) "ARIA role listitem is not allowed for given element", AND aria-required-children (CRITICAL, wcag2a/wcag131, 2 nodes) on .hero-tiles: "Element has children which are not allowed: a[aria-label]". The axe run had best-practice rules enabled (aria-allowed-role reports tags ["best-practice"]), so these are the real ruleset's findings.
findings.impact: The container claims role=list but contains an element that is not a listitem, so the list is malformed. Screen readers either drop the list semantics entirely or announce a wrong item count for the site's most-visited page. It is the only critical-impact axe violation on the whole site.
findings.suggestedFix: One fix kills both violations: delete `role="list"` from HeroTiles.astro:13 and `role="listitem"` from :20 (the anchors already carry self-describing aria-labels like "Sea Holly, flowering, view plant", so nothing is lost). If the group label is worth keeping, use real markup instead: `<ul class="hero-tiles">` with `<li>` grid items wrapping each `<a>`; do not keep the ARIA roles on the anchors.

findings.title: The home hero tiles explicitly delete the site's focus ring (outline: none) and replace it with a 2.0:1 border tint
findings.evidence: global.css:327 gives the whole site a good ring: `:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--bs-accent); outline-offset: 2px }` (measured 3.74:1 against the light page bg, 6.31:1 against the dark one, so it clears 1.4.11). global.css:464-466 then cancels it for the hero tiles: `.ht-tile:hover, .ht-tile:focus-visible { transform: translateY(-3px) scale(1.04); ... border-color: color-mix(in srgb, var(--bs-accent) 55%, var(--bs-line-2)); box-shadow: var(--bs-shadow-lg); outline: none; }`. That computed border is #83b28a light / #33774a dark, i.e. 1.96:1 (light) and 2.52:1 (dark) against the unfocused border it replaces. It is the ONLY `outline: none` in the stylesheet (grep -n outline global.css: lines 155, 327, 466, 682).
findings.impact: WCAG 2.4.7. The ~20 hero tiles are the first tab stops after the header on the home page, and focus and hover are styled identically, so a keyboard user gets a 1px tint change plus a 4% scale where every other control on the site gets a 2px green ring. Under prefers-reduced-motion the animation is off (global.css:487-490) but the transform still applies, so the cue survives; it is weak, not absent.
findings.suggestedFix: Delete the `outline: none` from global.css:466. Hover never draws an outline anyway, so removing it changes nothing visually except restoring the standard :focus-visible ring on keyboard focus. If the ring clashes with the 12px radius, add `.ht-tile:focus-visible { outline-offset: 3px }`.

findings.title: The 652 axe "incomplete" contrast nodes are all the translucent Foliage badge, and it can fall to 3.5:1
findings.evidence: I matched the incomplete counts exactly to the badge counts: /plants/ 172 incomplete = 172 `foliage-badge` in dist/plants/index.html; /plants/zone/5/ 125 = 125; /plants/traits/drought-tolerant/ 17 = 17; /guides/cool-border/ 1 = its 1 `gp-badge`. The badge (PlantCard.astro:68, global.css:206) is `color: var(--bs-muted)` at font-size .68rem (11.6px) / weight 600 over `background: color-mix(in srgb, var(--bs-surface) 80%, transparent)` on the card photo, so axe cannot resolve the background and returns incomplete. Computing the effective background as 80% surface + 20% photo: light theme #5C6B5A gives 5.67:1 over a white photo but 4.14:1 over a mid-green foliage photo and 3.53:1 over a black one; dark theme #A1B09B gives 8.00:1 over a dark photo but 4.84:1 over a pale one and 4.01:1 over white. AA needs 4.5:1 at this size.
findings.impact: An unknown subset of the 172 foliage cards on /plants/ (and 125 on the zone-5 hub, etc.) has badge text below the 4.5:1 AA floor. Which ones depends on the pixel content of each card photo behind the badge, which is exactly why axe abstains. The card photos are AI cards with widely varying backgrounds, so both failure directions are live.
findings.suggestedFix: Take the photo out of the equation instead of guessing per card: set `.foliage-badge { color: var(--bs-text); }` (global.css:206). That yields 9.28:1 in the light worst case and 7.79:1 in the dark worst case, so every badge clears AA regardless of the photo behind it. Raising the mix to `color-mix(... 92%, transparent)` would also work but keeps the text one step too faint.

findings.title: Lightbox: arrow keys and prev/next swap the photo and the credit with no announcement
findings.evidence: PlantGallery.astro:90-100 render() mutates `lbImg.src`, `lbImg.alt` and `lbCap.innerHTML` in place; :136-139 wire prev/next; :141-145 bind ArrowLeft/ArrowRight on document. Nothing in the panel is a live region (no aria-live anywhere in the component) and focus stays on whichever button was pressed. Also every photo on a page gets the SAME alt (`img alt={plant.commonName}`, :26), so even the alt would not differentiate them. 99 of the 135 gallery pages have 2 or more photos (counted in dist: 33 pages x2, 31 x3, 23 x4, 7 x5, 5 x6).
findings.impact: WCAG 4.1.3 Status Messages. A screen-reader user who opens the lightbox lands on the close button (correct), presses ArrowRight, and hears nothing: the image changed, the CC BY credit in the figcaption changed, and the AT was told none of it. The credit is a licence obligation, so the one place it is silently swapped is the one place it must not be.
findings.suggestedFix: Make the figure announce itself: add `aria-live="polite"` (and `aria-atomic="true"`) to `.lightbox-fig` (PlantGallery.astro:53), and in render() write a distinguishing alt, e.g. `lbImg.setAttribute("alt", it.alt + ", photo " + (idx+1) + " of " + items.length)`. Both are inside the existing dialog, so no focus juggling is needed.

findings.title: CLAUDE.md's "0 color-contrast violations in both themes" is true only for the default page state: the open listbox fails at 4.45:1
findings.evidence: axe color-contrast (serious, wcag2aa/wcag143), 1 node, LIGHT theme, /plants/ with a listbox open: `#f-month-opt-0 > .fs-opt-label` ("Any month", the aria-selected option) is #2f7a45 on #e9eee2 = 4.45:1, needs 4.5:1. Reproduced from tokens: --bs-accent-text #2F7A45 (global.css:68) applied by `.fs-opt[aria-selected="true"] { color: var(--bs-accent-text) }` (global.css:717), over `.fs-opt.fs-active { background: var(--bs-surface-3) }` #E9EEE2 (global.css:719) = 4.46:1 by my own computation. The dark equivalent (#6FCF8E on #222A23) is 7.72:1 and passes. CLAUDE.md "Known issues" says the 2026-07-14 fix left "axe-core: 0 color-contrast violations in both themes", which held only because nobody opened a dropdown.
findings.impact: The selected filter row, which is the one row the user is looking for, is the least legible row in the light-theme popup. It misses AA by 0.05. Second-order impact: the Known issues note is now inaccurate and will mislead the next agent into thinking contrast is closed.
findings.suggestedFix: Two lines, either of which clears it: (a) drop the accent tint on the selected row's label and rely on the check icon plus weight (`.fs-opt[aria-selected="true"] { color: var(--bs-text-strong); font-weight: 600 }`, 13.6:1); or (b) fix it as a side effect of the fs-active-option-invisible finding, since replacing the surface-3 tint with --bs-accent-weak + an inset accent ring changes the background under this text anyway. Whatever lands, amend the CLAUDE.md Known-issues line to say the claim covers the default state only.

findings.title: Every thumbnail in a gallery has the identical accessible name ("View a larger photo of Aster" x4)
findings.evidence: PlantGallery.astro:25 `aria-label={`View a larger photo of ${plant.commonName}`}` is the same string for every photo in the strip; the aria-label overrides the `` on :26 (so the alt is dead weight, though harmless). dist/plants/aster/index.html has 4 `.photo-thumb` links, all with that one name. 99 of 135 gallery pages have 2+ photos.
findings.impact: WCAG 2.4.4 Link Purpose. A screen-reader user pulling up the links list on /plants/aster/ sees four links with the same name and no way to tell them apart or to know which one they already opened.
findings.suggestedFix: Number them from the index that is already in the map: `aria-label={`View a larger photo of ${plant.commonName}, photo ${i + 1} of ${photos.length}`}` (PlantGallery.astro:25 already has `i` and `photos` in scope). Pairs naturally with the lightbox live-region fix.

findings.title: Companion photo links are tabindex=-1 but still in the accessibility tree, so screen readers hear every companion twice
findings.evidence:
PlantCompanions.astro:33-35: ``, immediately followed by :40 `

{c.commonName}

`. tabindex=-1 removes the link from the TAB order, not from the a11y tree, and the img alt gives it an accessible name. global.css:764-765 documents the intent ("skipped by the keyboard so each companion stays one tab stop") but that only solves half of it.
findings.impact: On a species plant page with 8 companions, a virtual-cursor user hears "link, Honeysuckle... heading level 3, link, Honeysuckle" 8 times, and the links list shows 16 entries for 8 destinations. It also creates a link a keyboard user can see highlighted on hover but can never reach.
findings.suggestedFix: Drop the wrapper and render the img directly (``), since the h3 link beside it already points at the same URL, so no internal link and no crawl path is lost. Do NOT just set alt="" while keeping the : that would leave a link with no accessible name and trade this for an axe link-name violation.

findings.title: The theme toggle announces "Toggle light or dark theme, pressed" without saying which state pressed means
findings.evidence: ThemeToggle.astro:3 `aria-label="Toggle light or dark theme"`; :17-21 sync() sets aria-pressed to the dark state. So a screen reader says "Toggle light or dark theme, toggle button, pressed" (dark) or "...not pressed" (light). Nothing in the name binds "pressed" to "dark". The sun/moon swap that tells sighted users the state is pure CSS (global.css:140-142) and says nothing to AT. Also note the button ships with no aria-pressed attribute in the HTML; it is only added by sync() on load, so with JS off the control is a dead button.
findings.impact: A screen-reader user cannot tell from the announcement whether the site is currently in dark mode or whether pressing will take them there. Not a WCAG failure (the state IS exposed), but the label does not name the state it is toggling.
findings.suggestedFix: Name the thing being toggled, not the action: `aria-label="Dark theme"` + the existing aria-pressed reads as "Dark theme, toggle button, pressed", which is unambiguous. Alternative: keep the label dynamic ("Switch to light theme" / "Switch to dark theme") and drop aria-pressed; do not do both.

findings.title: Guide FAQ questions are h3s with no section heading, so they nest under whatever h2 came last
