findings.evidence:
src/pages/guides/[slug].astro:110-119 renders the `qa` block as a bare `
` with `

{it.q}

` per item and no heading of its own. In dist/guides/cool-border/index.html the order is: `

Cool colour into fall

` ... `
` `

What flowers are blue and purple for a garden?

` x3, then `

Design a garden with these plants

`. Confirmed against the axe dom.headings dump for the guide page. This is not a skipped level (axe's heading-order rule ran, since best-practice rules were on, and passed).
findings.impact: Heading-navigation users get three FAQ questions presented as subsections of "Cool colour into fall", an unrelated planting section. The document outline misrepresents the page for exactly the users who navigate by outline.
findings.suggestedFix:
Emit a section heading before the questions in the `qa` branch of [slug].astro:110, e.g. `

Common questions

` (or use the block's existing optional `heading` field if guides.ts has one, as the `chips` branch at :103-109 does). Then the three h3s hang off their own h2.

findings.title: One hover animation escapes all nine prefers-reduced-motion blocks
findings.evidence: global.css:808 `.photo-thumb img { transition: transform .25s ease }` + :810 `.photo-thumb:hover img { transform: scale(1.05) }`. The nine reduce blocks (lines 94, 127, 195, 372, 487, 562, 606, 641, 722) cover html scroll-behavior, .btn, .card, .site-menu, .ht-tile/.hero-copy, .garden-preview (including its own gp-media img scale at :563), .pick, .guide-card and the fancy-select. The gallery thumbnail zoom is the only transform left. Note also that global.css:489 only kills the .ht-tile transition on :hover, so the focus-state transform still animates under reduced motion.
findings.impact: A user who has asked the OS for reduced motion still gets a 250ms 5% zoom on every gallery thumbnail they mouse over. Small, but it is the one gap in an otherwise complete policy.
findings.suggestedFix: Add `.photo-thumb:hover img { transform: none; }` to the existing reduce block (the garden-preview one at global.css:562-564 already has exactly this shape and is the natural home for it).

findings.title: scrollable-region-focusable on the open listbox is an axe false positive for the APG pattern; do not "fix" it
findings.evidence: axe (serious, wcag211/wcag213, 2 nodes, /plants/ only when a listbox is open): `ul.fs-pop#f-month-listbox[role=listbox][tabindex=-1]` "Element should be focusable". The rule wants tabindex>=0. But the element is programmatically focused the instant it becomes scrollable: plants/index.astro:308 `pop.focus()` inside api.open(), and it is `hidden` at every other moment (:219, :311-312). Keyboard operation is complete: ArrowDown/ArrowUp/Home/End/Enter/Space/Escape/Tab/typeahead are all bound on the ul (:332-342), and setActive() scrolls the active option into view (:261-264), so the scroll container is fully traversable by keyboard. This is the ARIA APG collapsible-dropdown-listbox pattern implemented correctly.
findings.impact: None. Complying with the rule literally (tabindex="0" on the ul) would ADD a spurious tab stop that lands on a hidden element in the tab order, which is a real regression. The genuine defect in this component is the invisible active-option highlight, filed separately.
findings.suggestedFix: No code change. If the axe run is ever gated in CI, suppress this rule for `.fs-pop` with a comment pointing at the APG pattern, and instead fix fs-active-option-invisible, which is the barrier this rule is a bad proxy for.

findings.title: BloomStrip's aria-label assumes a contiguous range; a second bloom window would read as one long range (0 cases today)
findings.evidence: BloomStrip.astro:9-12 builds the label from `on[0]` and `on[on.length-1]` only: `blooms ${MONTHS_LONG[on[0]]} to ${MONTHS_LONG[on[last]]}`. facets.ts:26-35 bloomMonthColors() loops over `p.bloom` as an ARRAY of windows, so a plant with two windows (say April-May and September-October) produces a gapped colour array whose label would read "blooms April to October". This is the same defect class as the known year-wrapping bug (Nov-Feb reads "January to December"), but it needs no wrap to trigger. I parsed all 1,677 bloom strips out of dist/plants/index.html and checked the 12 cells against each label: 0 non-contiguous sets and 0 year-wraps, so every label shipping today is accurate.
findings.impact: Nothing ships wrong today. It is a latent trap: the strip is role=img and the letters are aria-hidden, so this label is the ONLY thing a screen-reader user gets, and the first multi-window or winter-blooming plant the Studio publishes will silently lie to them on ~1,700 pages at once.
findings.suggestedFix: Make the label enumerate rather than assume: walk the 12 cells and join contiguous runs ("blooms April to May and September to October"), which fixes the wrap case and the gap case in the same helper. bloomShort() (facets.ts:39-44) has the identical first/last flaw and feeds the visible strip label, so fix them together.

findings.title: The 8 aria-valid-attr-value "incomplete" nodes are the four fs-triggers pointing aria-controls at a hidden listbox (not a defect)
findings.evidence: axe reports aria-valid-attr-value incomplete, 4 nodes per theme, on /plants/ and nowhere else. /plants/ is the only page with four visible controls carrying an IDREF ARIA attribute: the four fs-triggers (plants/index.astro:220 `trigger.setAttribute("aria-controls", pop.id)`), whose target `<ul class="fs-pop">` exists in the DOM but is `hidden` (:219). The header's nav-toggle also has aria-controls (Header.astro:21) but is `display:none` at desktop width (global.css:145), so axe excludes it, which is why no other page reports this. The artifact does not store node selectors for incomplete results, so the attribution is inferred from the counts, not read off.
findings.impact: None. ARIA requires the IDREF to resolve to an element, not to a visible one, and the referenced listbox is created and inserted before the attribute is set. axe abstains rather than failing.
findings.suggestedFix: No change. Worth recording so the next audit does not chase it.

findings.title: Home mobile LCP is 4,954 ms (POOR) because the actual LCP element is a lazy-loaded hero tile; the eager/high hint is spent on the wrong tile
findings.evidence: lighthouse-summary.json home/mobile: perf 82, lcp_ms 4954.2, lcp_score 0.27 - the only page in the CWV "poor" band (>4000 ms). lighthouse/home.mobile.json lcp-discovery-insight score=0, checklist priorityHinted=false, eagerlyLoaded=false, requestDiscoverable=true. lcp-breakdown-insight node = selector 'div.hero-visual > div.hero-tiles > a.ht-tile > img', nodeLabel 'Sea Holly', snippet shows loading="lazy" and no fetchpriority. DOM path '...,1,A,0,IMG' = the SECOND <a class="ht-tile">, i.e. tile index 1. Confirmed in dist/index.html: tile 0 = 'Big Sky Sunset Coneflower' (loading=eager fetchpriority=high, src cards/community/giant-coneflower.jpg), tile 1 = 'Sea Holly' (loading=lazy, fetchpriority=NONE, src cards-sm/sea-holly.webp). src/components/HeroTiles.astro:31-32 hardcodes `loading={i === 0 ? "eager" : "lazy"}` / `fetchpriority={i === 0 ? "high" : undefined}`. HeroTiles.astro:4-5 states the assumption in a comment: "the first tile's image is the LCP" - the measurement falsifies it. Observed LCP phases: TTFB 2 ms, resourceLoadDelay 102 ms, resourceLoadDuration 288 ms, elementRenderDelay 31 ms. All tiles are equal-size (global.css:458 aspect-ratio:1), so any first-row tile can win LCP; the grid is repeat(5,1fr) (global.css:457) and repeat(4,1fr) under 560px (global.css:509).
findings.impact: The home page - the site's front door and the page most likely to be the first organic landing - is the single worst Core Web Vitals result on the site and the ONLY page in the LCP "poor" band. LCP is a confirmed Google ranking signal, so this directly costs the SEO-first mission. Likely mechanism (inference, see confidence): tile 0 IS eager+high but is a ~102 KB community JPEG, while tile 1 is a small webp; equal-area LCP candidates do not replace one another, so the small lazy webp paints first and claims LCP, defeating the one priority hint on the page.
findings.suggestedFix: In src/components/HeroTiles.astro:31-32, make the whole first row eager rather than only tile 0, since all tiles are equal area and any of them can win LCP: `loading={i < 5 ? "eager" : "lazy"}` (5 = the desktop column count at global.css:457; it also covers the 4-column phone row at global.css:509). Keep fetchpriority="high" on i===0 only, or drop it. This is the same pattern PlantGrid already uses correctly (src/pages/plants/index.astro:58, `eagerCount={4}`, which emits loading=eager + fetchpriority=high on the first four cards). Also fix the now-false comment at HeroTiles.astro:4-5. Pair this with the community-JPEG fix (see hero-community-jpg-fallback) - together they are the single biggest perf win available.

findings.title: DOM bloat is not a byte problem, it is an LCP problem: on every card-grid page the LCP element is a text paragraph whose LCP is ~100% render-delay caused by parsing 20k-89k nodes
findings.evidence: lighthouse/*.json lcp-breakdown-insight: on plants-index, hub-zone, hub-trait(mobile) and bloom-combo the LCP element is the intro TEXT paragraph in header.listing-head (selector 'main#main > div.container > header.listing-head > p...'), with NO resourceLoadDelay/resourceLoadDuration phase at all - LCP is TTFB + elementRenderDelay only (hub-trait 10+135 ms, bloom-combo 13+126, hub-zone 21+126, plants-index 2+151, observed). mainthread-work-breakdown parseHTML scales cleanly with DOM: home 1,158 nodes -> 15 ms; hub-trait 20,105 -> 120 ms; bloom-combo 27,615 -> 170 ms; hub-zone 56,960 -> 322 ms; plants-index 89,370 -> 485 ms. Mobile FCP tracks it: baseline ~1,052 ms on the small pages (home/plant-detail/guide, 837-1,158 nodes) vs 1,205 (20k), 1,353 (27.6k), 1,505 (57k), 1,802 (89k) - roughly +8 to +11 ms of FCP per 1,000 DOM nodes. Mobile LCP tracks it too: 2,028 / 2,178 / 2,556 / 3,303 ms. plants-index mobile is also the only page with nonzero TBT (201 ms, right at the 200 ms warning line) and maxPotentialFID 251 ms, and its bootup-time detail shows scripting only 132 ms vs parseHTML 485 ms + styleLayout 246 ms - so the TBT is DOM cost, not JS cost. Lighthouse's own a11y gatherer hit PROTOCOL_TIMEOUT on plants-index in BOTH form factors (runtimeError.code PROTOCOL_TIMEOUT, artifactKey 'Accessibility'), so a11y/seo scores are null, not zero - the page is too big for the tooling. Per static-analysis.json breakdowns, the cost is uniform per card (~2,690 raw bytes/card): inlineSvg 1,540,401 B + specBlocks 1,978,361 B + bloomCells 751,593 B on /plants/index.html alone.
findings.impact: CLAUDE.md logs this as "[PERF-2/HUNT-9] /plants/index.html is heavy - ~4.45 MB raw", framed as a byte/size problem on one page. The runtime data reframes it twice over: (1) it is NOT a transfer problem (brotli takes /plants/ to 133 KB over the wire) and (2) it is NOT one page - it is every card-grid page type (hubs, zone/light/trait listings, bloom-calendar combos), i.e. essentially the entire SEO surface. The real cost is main-thread parse/style time landing squarely on LCP and FCP, the two metrics that feed Google ranking. hub-zone (2,556 ms) and plants-index (3,303 ms) are already in the "needs improvement" band purely from DOM weight, with no image or network involved.
findings.suggestedFix: The fix already named in CLAUDE.md is the right one and is now justified by runtime numbers: one <symbol> sprite for the repeated inline SVG icons (6,712 copies / 1.54 MB on /plants/) plus a gradient-painted bloom strip replacing the 20,124 per-cell spans (751 KB). Because the cost is per-card and uniform, the fix must land in the shared card component so all card-grid page types benefit, not in /plants/ alone. Do NOT scope this to /plants/. I have not applied or benchmarked the fix; the projected LCP/FCP win should be re-measured after.

findings.title: Plants whose card lives under cards/community/ have no cards-sm derivative, so full-size 82-130 KB JPEGs are served into 88x88 tiles and card thumbs
findings.evidence: src/components/HeroTiles.astro:15: `const img = p.cardSm?.url || p.card?.url;` - when cardSm is absent it falls back to the full-size card. dist/index.html hero tile 0 src = '.../plant-cutouts/cards/community/giant-coneflower.jpg' while tiles 1-4 are '.../cards-sm/*.webp'. Rendered box is 88x88 (lcp-breakdown-insight boundingRect width 88 height 88; global.css:458 aspect-ratio:1, :463 object-fit:cover). lighthouse home.mobile cache-insight/image-delivery-insight list these community JPEGs at 100-117 KB each: giant-coneflower.jpg totalBytes 102,346, orange-coneflower.jpg 111,118, rosa-damascena-damask-ro... 100,379, agastache-firebird.jpg 117,840, agastache-apricot-sprite 117,291. home/mobile image-delivery-insight: 'Est savings of 390 KiB', score 0. hub-trait/desktop: 'Est savings of 699 KiB'. Counted in dist/plants/index.html: 1,611 imgs use cards-sm/*.webp but 66 fall back to cards/community/*.jpg. Sitewide static-analysis.json images.byExtension: 54,461 webp vs 3,167 jpg (5.1% of 61,631 img tags). Every one of these images is also served Cache-Control: no-cache (cacheLifetimeMs 3600000 in cache-insight), the already-logged PERF-1.
findings.impact: A ~102 KB JPEG is decoded into an 88x88 CSS-pixel tile on the home page - roughly 30x the bytes needed. This is the mechanism that defeats the home page's one fetchpriority=high hint (see home-lcp-lazy-hero-tile) and it is the top line item in image-delivery-insight on every single page (390-699 KiB estimated savings). Only ~5% of images are affected, but they are disproportionately large and they land on the hero.
findings.suggestedFix: Root fix is Studio-side: publish cards-sm/*.webp derivatives for the community-photo plants so p.cardSm is populated for the whole catalog (same producer that owns PERF-1's cacheControl). Directory-side mitigation available today: in HeroTiles.astro:15, prefer plants that HAVE a cardSm for the hero grid (skip community-only plants when selecting the 19 tiles) so the home hero never serves a 100 KB JPEG into an 88px box. I have verified the fallback and the byte counts; I have not verified whether the Studio can regenerate cards-sm for these plants.

findings.title: 140 KB of fonts load on every page at High/VeryHigh priority ahead of the LCP image, and a third latin face loads that the code comment says should not
findings.evidence: resourceSummary in lighthouse-summary.json shows font count 3, transferSize 140,087 B - IDENTICAL on all 14 runs. lighthouse/home.mobile.json network-requests: fraunces-latin-opsz-normal.woff2 68,133 B priority=High; hanken-grotesk-latin-wght-normal.woff2 35,533 B priority=High; hanken-grotesk-latin-wght-italic.woff2 36,421 B priority=VeryHigh. Only the first two are preloaded (src/components/Layout.astro:80-81). The comment at Layout.astro:79 says the two preloaded faces are "the two latin faces every page uses; the rest lazy-load by unicode-range" - but the italic face is in the SAME latin unicode-range (global.css:52-53) and IS used on every card page, because botanical names are italic (global.css:215 `.card .botanical { font-style: italic }`, :282 `.detail-head .botanical`, :613 `.pick .bot`). So three latin faces load on every page, not two. Fonts are 140,087 of plant-detail mobile's 389,740 total transfer (36%) and of hub-zone mobile's 454,022 (31%) - by far the largest non-image asset class (the CSS is only 8,791 B). On home/mobile the LCP image shows resourceLoadDelay 102 ms, i.e. it is queued behind the CSS + these three fonts. Separately: Fraunces (67 KB, the single largest asset on a text page) is declared `font-weight: 100 900` (global.css:11,19) but every one of the 8 rules that consume var(--font-display) sets `font-weight: 600` (grep of global.css: 8/8 matches are `font-weight: 600`), so the entire 100-900 weight axis is never exercised.
findings.impact: First-visit only (public/_headers caches /fonts/* immutable for a year), but an SEO directory's organic traffic IS overwhelmingly first-visit, and this is exactly the traffic whose LCP Google measures. 140 KB of High/VeryHigh-priority font bytes compete with the LCP image for the mobile connection and contribute to the 102 ms resourceLoadDelay on home. NOTE the counter-intuitive part: preloading the italic face would make LCP WORSE, not better, by pulling more bytes ahead of the LCP image. The lever is fewer font bytes, not more preloads.
findings.suggestedFix: Two separable moves. (1) Fix the false comment at Layout.astro:79 - three latin faces load on every page, not two. (2) The byte lever: Fraunces is only ever rendered at weight 600, so pin the wght axis and re-subset, keeping the opsz axis (which IS used - optical sizing varies with heading size, and global.css:3-4 documents that intent). I did NOT inspect the woff2's actual variation axes or run a subsetter, so I cannot promise a number - verify the axes first, then measure. Do NOT add a third preload.

findings.title: Guide pages: mobile LCP 2,703 ms (needs improvement), bound entirely by LCP image download, not by priority hints
findings.evidence: lighthouse-summary.json guide/mobile: lcp_ms 2702.9, lcp_score 0.85. lcp-breakdown-insight: LCP element is the guide hero img ('Delphinium in bloom', selector 'div.container > article.guide-wrap > div.guide-hero-...'), phases TTFB 4 ms, resourceLoadDelay 7 ms, resourceLoadDuration 432 ms, elementRenderDelay 23 ms - i.e. 94% of the non-TTFB time is the image download itself. lcp-discovery-insight does NOT appear in guide/mobile failingPerfInsights, so the hero image IS already eager + priority-hinted (unlike the home hero). image-delivery-insight on guide/mobile: 'Est savings of 114 KiB', with /previews/cool-border.jpg totalBytes 127,141 / wastedBytes 90,803 and cards/delphinium.webp totalBytes 43,690 / wastedBytes 11,553.
findings.impact: Guides are the conversion funnel (guide_view -> preview_click -> design_click, per src/scripts/analytics.ts:6). They sit in the CWV "needs improvement" band on mobile for a reason that is purely image bytes - the markup and priority hints are already correct, so this is a clean, isolated win. /previews/cool-border.jpg alone wastes 90.8 KB at mobile render size, and the same pattern will repeat for every future garden-preview guide (the per-guide preview JPEG is composed by hand per CLAUDE.md).
findings.suggestedFix: Right-size the hand-composed preview JPEGs before upload to public/previews/ (cool-border.jpg is 127 KB serving a box that needs ~36 KB at mobile render size) and serve a webp alongside. Since CLAUDE.md documents these as hand-composed per guide, add the target dimensions to that workflow so the next guide (the hot border) does not repeat it. Separately, cards/delphinium.webp is a full-size card where a cards-sm derivative would do - same root cause as hero-community-jpg-fallback.

findings.title: script-src requires 'unsafe-inline' because 100% of the site's JS is inline; the CSP therefore provides no XSS protection for scripts
findings.evidence: public/_headers:32: `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://static.cloudflareinsights.com`. Verified this is architecturally forced: dist/ contains ZERO .js/.mjs files (`find dist -name '*.js' -o -name '*.mjs'` returns 0), resourceSummary type=script is count 0 / transferSize 0 on all 14 Lighthouse runs, and every script is inline - dist/index.html has 6 script tags (theme no-flash 687 B, 2x ld+json, nav toggle 1,374 B, another 1,160 B, and Astro's module 369 B locally / 2,008 B live). static-analysis.json breakdowns: /plants/index.html parts.inlineScripts count 6, bytes 17,869; card-grid hub pages count 4, bytes 3,672. The _headers comment (lines 15-17) is honest about this: "Inline scripts (the theme no-flash snippet, the /plants/ client-side filters) and inline styles keep 'unsafe-inline'."
findings.impact: With 'unsafe-inline' present, script-src is effectively inert as an XSS control - an injected <script> would execute. Practical risk here is LOW: the site is static, has no auth, no forms, no user input, Astro auto-escapes interpolations, and src/lib/seo.ts's ldJson() specifically guards JSON-LD against a </script> breakout from catalog text. But catalog text DOES flow from an external JSON feed into HTML on 1,866 pages, so this is real defence-in-depth that has been traded away. Worth logging honestly rather than leaving implicit.
findings.suggestedFix: Accept the tradeoff (the inline-everything design is correct for performance - see the alreadyFine notes) but log it. If you ever want script-src back: emit sha256 hashes for each inline script at build and generate _headers rather than shipping it static (public/_headers is currently a static file copied by Astro). Astro 5 has experimental CSP hash generation. This is NOT a perf change and must not be used as an argument for adding an external bundle.

findings.title: Layout.astro's analytics comment is wrong on two counts: the module is inlined (not fingerprinted) and is 2,008 B live (not ~1 KB)
findings.evidence: src/components/Layout.astro:117-118 comment: "Bundled + fingerprinted by Astro, deferred, ~1 KB". Verified against reality: dist/ contains NO .js file at all, so nothing is fingerprinted - Astro inlines the module directly into every HTML page as <script type="module">. Live bloomseye.com home ships that module inline at 2,008 bytes (fetched and measured), i.e. 2x the documented size. "deferred" IS correct (type=module defers by default). Live guide page ships 9,291 B of total inline script vs home's 6,051 B.
findings.impact: Minor doc drift, but it matters for two reasons: (1) "fingerprinted" implies the analytics code is cached once across navigations - it is not, it is re-sent inside every one of 1,866 HTML documents; (2) it is one of the reasons script-src needs 'unsafe-inline'. Neither is a perf problem at 2 KB (brotli handles it), but the comment misleads the next person sizing the JS budget.
findings.suggestedFix: Correct the comment at src/components/Layout.astro:117-118 to say the module is inlined into each page (not fingerprinted) and is ~2 KB. No code change needed - the inlining is fine at this size.

findings.title: The site's only layout shift (CLS 0.0026 on /plants/) traces to the fancy-select swap guarding WIDTH but not HEIGHT
findings.evidence: Exactly ONE layout shift exists across all 14 runs. lighthouse/plants-index.mobile.json layout-shifts: 1 item, score 0.002648, node selector 'body > main#main > div.container > div#plant-results' (the results container, boundingRect top 545, height 300,081). Every other run has cls 0 and layout-shifts items=[]. The width guard IS present and IS working: src/pages/plants/index.astro:69-71 sets `.filters select { min-width: 9rem }` with the comment "Match the enhanced .fs-trigger width so the fancy-select swap cannot re-wrap the filter row", and global.css:674 confirms `.fs-trigger { ... min-width: 9rem; }` with matching font-size .95rem and padding .6rem .8rem. But global.css:676 also sets `line-height: 1.2` on .fs-trigger, while the `.filters input, .filters select` rule (plants/index.astro:64-68) sets font-family/font-size/padding and NO line-height. The shifted node is the container directly BELOW the filter row, which is what a filter-row height change would move.
findings.impact: Effectively zero: 0.0026 against a 0.1 CLS budget is a 38x margin, and CLS is 0 on every other page. Reporting it only so the planning session does not spend time hunting a phantom, and to correct the record: the min-width guard the code comment describes IS doing its job (no re-wrap); what is unguarded is the box HEIGHT.
findings.suggestedFix: If you want it at exactly zero: add `line-height: 1.2;` to the `.filters input, .filters select` rule at src/pages/plants/index.astro:64-68 so the native <select> box height matches the .fs-trigger button that replaces it (global.css:676). I did NOT isolate this causally (no before/after run) - the shifted node and the CSS mismatch are both verified, but the causal link is inferred. Given the 38x margin this is optional.

findings.title: The universal :focus-visible rule silently overwrites border-radius on .btn, .icon-btn, .card and .chip
findings.evidence: src/styles/global.css:327 declares `:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--bs-accent); outline-offset: 2px; border-radius: 4px; }`. `border-radius: 4px` is a property of the ELEMENT, not the outline. `:focus-visible` has specificity (0,1,0), identical to `.btn` (global.css:121, `border-radius: var(--radius-pill)` = 999px), `.icon-btn` (138, pill), `.card` (193, `var(--radius)` = 14px) and `.chip` (250, pill) - and it comes LATER in source, so it wins. Confirmed in the shipped bundle dist/_astro/Layout.BDtOC_yW.css by byte offset: `.btn{` at 4741, `.icon-btn{` at 6304, `.card{` at 8836, `.chip{` at 11011, and `}:focus-visible{outline:2px solid var(--bs-accent);outline-offset:2px;border-radius:4px}` at 15020. Elements defined AFTER 327 are unaffected (.fs-trigger at 31123, .filter-toggle at 37918, .photo-thumb, .guide-card, .lightbox-btn), and `.searchbox input` (global.css:154) survives because it is (0,1,1).
findings.impact: Every keyboard user watching focus move sees pill-shaped controls snap into 4px rectangles: 'Open the app' and 'Design a garden with this' (.btn-primary), 'Clear' (.btn-ghost), the Amazon button (.btn-amazon, which inherits .btn's radius), the theme toggle and hamburger (.icon-btn), every facet chip, and every plant card (14px -> 4px). Not a WCAG failure (a 2px accent ring is still drawn), but it is a visible defect on the exact interaction path that only keyboard and switch users take, and it makes the focus state read as a rendering bug.
findings.suggestedFix: Delete `border-radius: 4px` from the `:focus-visible` rule at src/styles/global.css:327. Chrome, Firefox and Safari all draw `outline` following the element's own `border-radius`, so the declaration buys nothing and costs the four rules above. Verify by tabbing the home hero CTA and a plant card in both themes.

findings.title: The /plants/ listbox keyboard cursor (.fs-opt.fs-active) is a 1.08:1 background tint - effectively invisible
findings.evidence: src/styles/global.css:719 `.fs-opt.fs-active { background: var(--bs-surface-3); }` is the ONLY visual indicator of the roving keyboard position inside the popup, whose own background is `var(--bs-surface-2)` (global.css:699). Computed: light #E9EEE2 on #F3F6EE = 1.08:1; dark #222A23 on #1C231D = 1.09:1. WCAG 1.4.11 wants 3:1 for a state indicator; WCAG 2.4.7 wants a visible focus indicator. The listbox uses aria-activedescendant (src/pages/plants/index.astro:260), so the browser's real focus ring sits on the <ul> as a whole (via the universal :focus-visible), never on the active option. This is also the root of axe finding #5: `.fs-opt[aria-selected="true"] { color: var(--bs-accent-text) }` (global.css:717) puts #2F7A45 on that same #E9EEE2 active background = 4.46:1 by my own computation (axe measured 4.45:1), just under the 4.5:1 AA floor.
findings.impact: A sighted keyboard user pressing ArrowDown through the 13-option bloom-month listbox on /plants/ cannot see which option they are on. The keyboard handling itself is correct (arrow/Home/End/typeahead all bound at src/pages/plants/index.astro:332-342, pop.focus() at 308), so the interaction is functional but unnavigable by sight. This also means CLAUDE.md's '0 color-contrast violations in both themes' is true only for the DEFAULT page state, not for the open listbox.
findings.suggestedFix: Give `.fs-opt.fs-active` (global.css:719) an indicator that clears 3:1 against the popup's --bs-surface-2 background - e.g. `background: var(--bs-accent-weak); box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 2px var(--bs-accent);` - instead of the near-invisible --bs-surface-3 fill. That simultaneously fixes the 4.46:1 selected-label contrast, because --bs-accent-text would no longer sit on --bs-surface-3. Do NOT 'fix' axe's scrollable-region-focusable by adding tabindex=0 to the <ul>: that would add a bogus tab stop and break the APG combobox pattern.

findings.title: Prose links outside the guide system have no underline and rely on colour alone (WCAG 1.4.1)
findings.evidence:
src/styles/global.css:101 sets `a { text-decoration: none; }` globally. The underline rule that restores it is scoped to guides only: global.css:571-573 `.guide-body p a, .guide-body li a, .qa a, .callout a { text-decoration: underline; ... }`. So a link inside body prose anywhere else is distinguished from its surrounding text by colour alone. Measured foreground-vs-surrounding-text ratios (3:1 required by the technique axe applies): inside `

` - dark #6FCF8E link vs #A1B09B text = 1.19:1, light #2F7A45 vs #5C6B5A = 1.08:1; inside normal body text - light #2F7A45 vs #1E2A22 = 2.83:1, dark #6FCF8E vs #E9EEE7 = 1.62:1. Confirmed instances: src/components/PlantGallery.astro:20 (`How photos are sourced` inside `

`, the 4 nodes axe flagged), src/pages/credits.astro:22 and :24, plus 4 in-paragraph links in src/pages/privacy.astro and 2 in src/pages/terms.astro.
findings.impact: Colour-blind and low-vision readers cannot find the links. This is on every plant page that has photos (the /plants/ 'Has photos' filter counts 135) plus /credits/, /privacy/ and /terms/ - i.e. the legal and attribution pages, where the link IS the remedy the page promises (the credits page's 'email support@bloomseye.com' correction path). The existing `.photo-credit a` and `.lightbox-cap a` (global.css:812, 829) already underline, which shows the intent was there; the note paragraph and the legal pages were simply missed.
findings.suggestedFix:
Widen the underline rule at src/styles/global.css:571-573 to cover prose links outside .guide-body, e.g. add `.photo-gallery-note a, .article p a, .intel-xref a` and a rule for the legal/credits pages' paragraph links (check each page's actual wrapper element before writing the selector - credits.astro/privacy.astro/terms.astro use plain

inside their own containers). Reuse the same declaration block (underline + text-underline-offset: 2px + the accent-mix decoration colour) so the look matches the guides.

findings.title: Bloom-strip filled cells are indistinguishable from empty cells for pale blooms: 72% of light-theme cards under 3:1
findings.evidence: On card grids the bloom strip is the ONLY visual conveyance of bloom months (src/components/PlantCard.astro:76-86 shows sun/zones/height/water, never a bloom range; only the detail page states it in text, src/pages/plants/[slug].astro:129). Filled vs empty is a pure fill-colour difference: filled cells carry an inline `background:<hex>` (BloomStrip.astro:17,24), empty cells take the track. Measured over the 1,505 cards in dist/plants/index.html that have a strip: LIGHT theme (empty track #D2DBC3, set at global.css:183) - 1,088 cards (72%) have every filled cell under 3:1 against the track; worst hexes #f4f2ea (67 cards, 1.28:1), #fbfbfb (13, 1.38:1), #f2f0e8 (13, 1.26:1), #f4c020 (8, 1.18:1). DARK theme (--bs-track #2A332A) - 406 cards (27%) under 3:1; worst #d6283a (10, 2.63:1), #7a3a9e (7, 1.82:1). The `box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 1px rgba(21,32,25,.20)` added at global.css:183 does NOT help: the selector is `.bloomstrip .cell`, so it outlines filled AND empty cells identically.
findings.impact: WCAG 1.4.11. A low-vision or colour-blind sighted user cannot read which months a white, cream, pale-pink or pale-yellow plant blooms in - on /plants/, on every hub, on every bloom-calendar combo, and on the home grid. Screen-reader users ARE covered (BloomStrip.astro:16,23 give the strip role=img with an aria-label spelling out the range), so this is squarely a sighted-low-vision gap. It is the site's signature element, so the failure lands on the primary value proposition.
findings.suggestedFix: Make filled-vs-empty a difference of FORM, not just fill. Concretely: at src/styles/global.css:183, stop giving every cell the same inset ring; scope a much stronger ring to filled cells only - filled cells are exactly the ones that carry an inline style attribute, so `.bloomstrip .cell[style] { box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 1px rgba(21,32,25,.55); }` selects them - and leave empty cells as a flat track with no ring. That gives every filled cell a hard dark edge regardless of how pale the bloom hex is. Needs a matching dark-theme rule (dark currently has no ring at all, which is why 27% of dark cards fail). Verify against a white-bloom plant (any card with #fbfbfb) in both themes before shipping.

findings.title: .ht-tile deliberately removes the site-wide focus ring and substitutes a 1px border-colour change
findings.evidence: src/styles/global.css:464-466: `.ht-tile:hover, .ht-tile:focus-visible { transform: translateY(-3px) scale(1.04); z-index: 3; ... border-color: color-mix(in srgb, var(--bs-accent) 55%, var(--bs-line-2)); box-shadow: var(--bs-shadow-lg); outline: none; }`. This is the ONLY place in the whole stylesheet that cancels the universal `:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--bs-accent) }` at global.css:327. The replacement indicator is a 1px border-colour change plus a shadow plus a lift - and the tile's interior is a full-bleed photo (HeroTiles.astro:25-34), so that 1px border is the only edge signal.
findings.impact: The 38 hero tiles on the home page (the first 38 tab stops after the header) are the one place on the site where keyboard focus does not draw the standard 2px accent ring. The substitute is visible but weaker and inconsistent with every other control, and it is identical to the hover state, so focus and hover are indistinguishable.
findings.suggestedFix: Split the selector at global.css:464 so the :focus-visible branch keeps the universal outline: leave `.ht-tile:hover { transform/box-shadow/border-color }` as-is and drop `outline: none` from the :focus-visible branch (or replace it with an explicit `outline: 2px solid var(--bs-accent); outline-offset: 2px;`). The tile has `overflow: hidden` (global.css:458), which clips inner content but NOT its own outline, so an outset ring will render.

findings.title: One motion effect escapes all nine prefers-reduced-motion blocks: the gallery thumbnail zoom
findings.evidence: The nine `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` blocks are at src/styles/global.css:94, 127, 195, 372, 487, 562, 606, 641, 722. Not covered: global.css:808 `.photo-thumb img { ... transition: transform .25s ease; }` plus :810 `.photo-thumb:hover img { transform: scale(1.05); }`. Every other transform-on-hover in the file IS nulled by one of those blocks (.btn :127, .card :195, .garden-preview + .gp-media img + .gp-launch :562-564, .pick :606, .guide-card :641), and both keyframe entrances are nulled (:488 tile-in/hero-rise, :724 fs-pop-in). Secondary gap: the block at :487-490 nulls `.ht-tile:hover { transition: none }` but NOT `.ht-tile:focus-visible`, so the focus lift (translateY(-3px) scale(1.04), global.css:464) still animates over 200 ms under reduced motion.
findings.impact: A user with vestibular sensitivity who has set prefers-reduced-motion still gets an animated 5% zoom on every real-photo thumbnail they hover on a plant page, and an animated lift when tabbing the home hero tiles. Small effects, but they are the two the stylesheet's own discipline missed.
findings.suggestedFix: Add `.photo-thumb:hover img { transform: none; }` to the reduced-motion block at src/styles/global.css:722-725 (the nearest existing one to the gallery rules), and change `.ht-tile:hover { transition: none; }` at :489 to `.ht-tile:hover, .ht-tile:focus-visible { transition: none; }` so the focus lift snaps instead of animating.

findings.title: The photo-credit link is ~16.5 CSS px tall, under the WCAG 2.2 AA 24x24 target-size floor
findings.evidence: src/styles/global.css:811-812: `.photo-credit { margin-top: .5rem; font-size: .72rem; line-height: 1.35; }` and `.photo-credit a { ... }`. At the 17px body root (global.css:97) that is 12.24px x 1.35 = ~16.5px of line box, which is the link's full clickable height. In src/components/PlantGallery.astro:28-32 the link is the ENTIRE <figcaption> content (the whole attribution string), so the WCAG 2.2 2.5.8 'inline in a sentence of non-target text' exception is thin. The spacing exception also looks unavailable: the 24px undisturbed circle would overlap the .photo-thumb link, which sits only .5rem (8.5px) above. Nothing else on the site is under 24px: .icon-btn is 40x40 (global.css:138, 36x36 at <=560px per :358), .fs-opt has min-height 2.5rem (:712), .lightbox-btn is 44x44 (:830-834), and .filter-toggle / .fs-trigger / .chip / .btn all compute to >=38px tall. axe did not flag this - target-size was not in the ruleset that ran.
findings.impact: Motor-impaired and touch users can mis-tap the credit link (which is legally load-bearing: the CC BY licence requires the attribution link) and hit the thumbnail lightbox instead. Affects every plant page with photos (135 plants).
findings.suggestedFix: Raise the credit link's hit area without changing the visual type size: add `.photo-credit a { display: inline-block; padding-block: 4px; }` at src/styles/global.css:812 and increase the `.photo-credit { margin-top }` from .5rem so the 24px circles of the credit link and the thumbnail above it no longer intersect. Check the 168px mobile tile width (global.css:856-858) still lays out cleanly.

findings.title: Sticky header runs a full-width backdrop-filter blur on every scroll frame, including on the 89k-node /plants/ page
findings.evidence: src/styles/global.css:130: `.site-header { position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 50; background: color-mix(in srgb, var(--bs-header) 88%, transparent); backdrop-filter: saturate(140%) blur(10px); ... }`. A translucent sticky element with backdrop-filter forces the compositor to re-snapshot and re-blur the content behind it as that content scrolls. On /plants/ that content is 89,370 DOM nodes (Lighthouse), the page whose mobile TBT is already 201 ms. Secondary: `.card .thumb .foliage-badge { ... backdrop-filter: blur(4px); }` (global.css:206) - 172 such nodes exist in dist/plants/index.html (grep count), each its own backdrop root, though `content-visibility: auto` on .card (global.css:203) means offscreen ones are skipped. Two more instances, both rare: .gp-badge (:548) and .lightbox-btn (:834).
findings.impact: Scroll jank / dropped frames on mid-range mobile on exactly the pages that are already heaviest. UNVERIFIED as a measured cost: I did not profile it, and none of the existing artifacts would have caught it - Lighthouse's TBT measures main-thread blocking during LOAD, not compositor cost during SCROLL.
findings.suggestedFix: Do not change this blind. Measure first: record a Chrome performance trace while scrolling dist/plants/index.html on a 4x-throttled CPU, with and without the backdrop-filter declaration on line 130, and compare dropped frames. If it is expensive, the cheapest fix is an opaque header (`background: var(--bs-header)`, dropping the color-mix and the backdrop-filter) - the header sits on --bs-bg/--bs-surface, which are near-identical to --bs-header in both themes, so the visual delta is small.

findings.title: One 38.5 KB render-blocking stylesheet ships to all 1,867 pages, ~40% of it route-specific
findings.evidence: dist/_astro/Layout.BDtOC_yW.css is 38,565 bytes (static-analysis.json nonHtmlAssets), linked render-blocking from every page's <head> (`<link rel="stylesheet" href="/_astro/Layout.BDtOC_yW.css">` in dist/index.html). Lighthouse render-blocking-insight puts it at ~150 ms on mobile (8.8 KB brotli). It is one file because src/components/Layout.astro:2 does `import "../styles/global.css"`. Route-specific blocks inside it: the fancy-select listbox, global.css:655-725 (/plants/ only); the guides system (guide-head / hero / garden-preview / callout / pick / qa / guide-card), :514-653 (guides only); deep-intel + companions, :727-782 (species plant pages only); the photo gallery + lightbox, :784-859 (plant pages with photos only). That is roughly lines 514-859 of 859 - about 40% of the source file - carried by pages that never use it, including the home page.
findings.impact: ~150 ms of render-blocking on mobile on every page, a slice of which is CSS the page cannot use. A contributor to the home/mobile perf score of 82 (worst of the 9 Lighthouse runs), though the dominant home-page cost is the LCP hero-tile priority bug, not this.
findings.suggestedFix: Move the route-scoped blocks out of global.css into per-page `<style>` blocks in the pages that own them - Astro scopes and code-splits those automatically, and src/pages/plants/index.astro:62-74 already does exactly this for the .filters rules. Candidates by size: the guides block (global.css:514-653) into src/pages/guides/[slug].astro, the gallery+lightbox block (:784-859) into PlantGallery.astro, the fancy-select block (:655-725) into src/pages/plants/index.astro. Watch out: several of those classes are also emitted by SHARED components (.chip, .callout, .bloomstrip-wrap), so grep each class against dist/ before moving it. Do this after the LCP fix, not instead of it.

findings.title: Mark.astro is dead (confirms CDH-2), plus five dead CSS rules, one fully-superseded block, and four unreferenced tokens
findings.evidence: Verified by extracting every `class="..."` token from all 1,867 built pages in dist/ AND from every .astro/.ts file in src/, then diffing against every class selector in global.css. (1) src/components/Mark.astro is imported by nothing - grep for `Mark` across src/ returns only the file itself; its companion rule `.brand .mark` (global.css:134) is therefore dead too. Confirms CLAUDE.md CDH-2. (2) Class selectors present in no built page and no source file: `.prose` (global.css:111), `.card .strip-label` (:246), `.year-ribbon .yr-label` (:159), `.band--surface` (:401), `.band--accent` (:402) - only `.band--alt` is actually used (src/pages/index.astro:132, 156, 199). (3) The `.year-ribbon .yr-cells` / `.col` / `.col span` block at global.css:160-162 is entirely superseded by the identical selectors at :493-496 (later wins). (4) Tokens declared in BOTH themes and referenced zero times (`grep -ro 'var(--<token>)' src/ | wc -l` returns 0 for each): `--bs-price`, `--bs-accent-strong`, `--bs-overlay`, `--bs-header-text`.
findings.impact: Small byte cost in the render-blocking stylesheet; the real cost is that the next agent reading global.css cannot tell which rules are load-bearing. One latent trap worth removing: `--bs-accent-strong` in dark (#3BB869) against `--bs-on-accent` (#FFFFFF) is 2.55:1 - if anyone reaches for it as a button background it fails AA badly. Better deleted than left as a tempting-looking token.
findings.suggestedFix: Delete src/components/Mark.astro and global.css:134; delete global.css:111, :159, :246, :401, :402; delete the superseded block at global.css:160-162; delete the four unused token declarations from both :root (:63-80) and :root[data-theme="dark"] (:81-90). Do NOT delete --bs-track, --bs-danger, --bs-warn or --bs-shadow-lg (3, 2, 2 and 5 live references respectively). Re-run the dist/ class diff after the change to confirm nothing regressed.

findings.title: Three code comments still claim --bs-faint fails AA; after the 2026-07-14 token fix it does not
findings.evidence: src/styles/global.css:297-298 says '--bs-faint on this background is 2.9:1, under the 4.5:1 AA floor'; :747-748 repeats 'faint misses WCAG AA on the tinted callouts'; :323-324 makes the same argument for the affiliate note. The actual light-theme --bs-faint is now #646B5B (global.css:66): 5.53:1 on #FFFFFF, 5.13:1 on --bs-bg #F5F7F2, 5.07:1 on --bs-surface-2, 4.69:1 on --bs-surface-3 - AA on every surface it is used with. Dark --bs-faint #8B9786 (:84) ranges 6.27:1 down to 4.83:1. The 2.9:1 figure was the PRE-fix value.
findings.impact: The styling choices those comments defend (using --bs-muted for the '(paid link)' note, the Amazon affiliate statement, and the intel source credits) are still correct and should NOT change. But the stated reason is now false, so a future agent will either 'correct' the comment wrongly or distrust the whole token audit. Comment debt, not a rendering bug.
findings.suggestedFix: Reword the three comments (global.css:297-298, 323-324, 747-748) to say the disclosure/credit text is deliberately one step darker than the caption token for legibility, rather than asserting a contrast ratio that is no longer true. Do not change any declaration.

alreadyFine.what: `content-visibility: auto; contain-intrinsic-size: auto 340px` is ALREADY SHIPPED on `.card` and is demonstrably working. Item 5 of my brief ("this may be the highest value-to-effort fix on the whole site") is already done. Do not plan it.
alreadyFine.evidence: src/styles/global.css:203 (`.card { content-visibility: auto; contain-intrinsic-size: auto 340px; }`, with the rationale in the comment at :196-202), and it survives to the build: dist/_astro/Layout.BDtOC_yW.css contains `.card{content-visibility:auto;contain-intrinsic-size:auto 340px}` as the LAST .card rule, so nothing overrides it. Proof it works, from the Lighthouse artifacts: styleLayout is FLAT and completely uncorrelated with page size - 174 ms (plant-detail, 837 nodes), 262 ms (guide, 1,066), 387 ms (home, 1,158), 249 ms (hub-trait, 20,105), 241 ms (bloom-combo, 27,615), 267 ms (hub-zone, 56,960), 246 ms (plants-index, 89,370). Regression of styleLayout on raw MB: slope -5.2 ms/MB, R^2 = 0.0173, i.e. ZERO relationship. The 89,370-node page has LOWER styleLayout than the 1,158-node home page. By contrast parseHTML over the same 7 pages regresses at 111.9 ms/MB with R^2 = 0.9990. Style and layout cost has been fully decoupled from card count; only parse and DOM-construction cost still scale.

alreadyFine.what: The /plants/ client-side filter is already proven compatible with content-visibility, so no fix in this package endangers it.
alreadyFine.evidence: The filter (src/pages/plants/index.astro:87-111) only calls getAttribute() and sets style.display; it never reads layout (no offsetTop/getBoundingClientRect), so it cannot force the skipped subtrees to render. Empirically confirmed in the audit run cited in my brief: with content-visibility live, the "Has photos" toggle correctly showed 135 cards and announced "135 plants" via aria-live. CLS on plants-index mobile is 0.0026, so `contain-intrinsic-size: auto 340px` is a good estimate and is not causing scroll jank.

alreadyFine.what: No horizontal overflow and no image-attribute gaps on card grids: every plant card ships width, height, loading and decoding.

Removing data-name would silently regress mobile card padding. It is also the most expensive single attr to compress (-13.3% brotli on /plants/), which makes it a tempting and wrong target.

alreadyFine.evidence: static-analysis.json perCardRawBytes: /plants/ 2,693; /plants/light/full-sun/ 2,692; /plants/zone/8/ 2,683; /plants/zone/5/ 2,682; /bloom-calendar/zone-5/june/ 2,722; /bloom-calendar/zone-8/july/ 2,725; /plants/traits/drought-tolerant/ 2,743. alreadyFine.what: The footer logo's missing loading="lazy" costs nothing. It is NOT a real finding.

alreadyFine.what: The photo gallery is correctly a secondary, deferred block and never the hero: it does not touch LCP on any plant page.

alreadyFine.what: THE KEY QUESTION, answered definitively: the fancy-select's no-JS fallback is SAFE. If the enhancer never runs, the four native <select> controls stay visible and fully operable.

alreadyFine.what: The single 38.5 KB stylesheet should NOT be split and should NOT be inlined. The numbers do not support it.

alreadyFine.what: The /plants/ filter path has no CSS layout-thrash pathology - the 201 ms TBT is grid re-placement, not a stylesheet bug

(2) No :nth-child, :has(), or sibling combinator anywhere touches .card - a grep of global.css finds only `.pick-list > .pick:last-child:nth-child(odd)` (:600, guide pages) and `.photo-strip:has(> .photo-item:only-child)` (:800-801, plant-detail pages); neither can invalidate a .card sibling. (3) The filter JS reads only attributes (getAttribute) inside the loop - no offsetHeight / getBoundingClientRect - so there is no forced synchronous layout, and it guards every write with `if (c.style.display !== disp)` (src/pages/plants/index.astro:106). (4) `.card { content-visibility: auto; contain-intrinsic-size: auto 340px; }` (global.css:203) actively HELPS, skipping layout and paint for offscreen cards. Nothing in the CSS needs to change to make the filter faster.

I priced the fixes by SIMULATING them on the real built HTML (rewriting dist/plants/index.html in memory and re-measuring raw bytes, brotli q5, and element count). The byte and node numbers are therefore measured, not estimated. The MILLISECOND figures are extrapolations from the 7-page regression (111.9 ms/MB parseHTML, R^2 = 0.999) and are UNVERIFIED until the fixes are actually built and re-run through Lighthouse. Treat the ms as forecasts.

My simulation of the sprite assumed an INLINE <symbol> block per page (required so `stroke="currentColor"` still picks up `color: var(--bs-accent-text)` from global.css:222 - cross-document <use> does not reliably inherit currentColor in Safari). I did not render it in a browser to confirm the icons still paint correctly at 13px and at the 10px mobile override (global.css:242). That needs a screenshot before it ships.

Is the Cloudflare AI-crawler block INTENTIONAL? It is a business decision, not a bug, and I found evidence both ways: src/pages/terms.astro explicitly forbids using the content to "build, train, or improve a machine-learning model, an AI system, or a competing dataset" (so ai-train=no is consistent), but src/lib/seo.ts:76 says the plant structured data exists "for comprehension + AI answer engines". Someone has to decide, and then the repo has to say so.

Should ClaudeBot and CCBot specifically stay blocked? They are the two entries with the most real AI-visibility cost. The answer-engine fetchers (OAI-SearchBot, ChatGPT-User, Claude-SearchBot, Claude-User, PerplexityBot) are NOT blocked, so live AI retrieval still works, but CCBot removes the site from Common Crawl, which feeds a long tail of downstream tools. I could not determine from the artifacts whether Anthropic serves Claude's search results from ClaudeBot's crawl or from Claude-SearchBot's.

Does `Google-Extended: Disallow: /` affect AI Overviews or only Gemini training/grounding? Google's own docs say Google-Extended does not affect Search or AI Overviews inclusion (those follow Googlebot), but I did not verify that against the current documentation. If it does affect AI Overviews, the severity of the robots finding rises sharply. UNVERIFIED.

For the 3 Anemone blanda duplicate pairs: which slug is canonical? The anemone-* pages say zones 5-9 / part shade, the windflower-* pages say zones 4-8 / part sun. Someone has to say which is correct before either a 301 (directory) or a dedupe (Studio) can be done, otherwise the redirect just picks a winner at random.

For the bare-epithet H1 fix: several groupNames compose awkwardly when prepended ("Dahlias 'Cafe au Lait'", "English (David Austin) Roses 'Abraham Darby'"). Is the display name best composed from groupName (searchable, but needs a plural/parenthetical guard) or from the genus parsed out of botanicalName (always clean, but Latin-only and less searchable)? Worth looking at the distinct groupName list before committing to one.

The foliage-badge contrast range (3.53:1 to 5.67:1 light, 4.01:1 to 8.00:1 dark) is computed from the worst/best possible photo behind the 80%-opaque badge. Nobody has sampled the actual top-left pixels of the 172 foliage card images to say HOW MANY badges really fail. The suggested fix (--bs-text) makes the question moot, so I would not spend the sampling effort; but if you want the count before changing a token, that is the missing measurement.

The 8 aria-valid-attr-value "incomplete" nodes are attributed to the four fs-triggers by elimination (4 per theme, /plants/ only, and it is the only page with four visible IDREF-carrying controls). axe-results.json does not store node selectors for incomplete results, so this is inference, not observation. Re-running axe with incomplete node details would confirm it in one shot.

Whether axe-core 4.12's `target-size` rule was actually enabled in this run. It reported no violations and my CSS measurements agree that everything clears 24x24, but I could not confirm from the artifacts that the rule ran at all, so "no target-size violations" is weaker evidence than it looks.

The lightbox relies on aria-modal=true alone: nothing outside the dialog is `inert` or aria-hidden. Current NVDA/JAWS/VoiceOver honour aria-modal and confine the virtual cursor, so I did not file it as a defect. If the site needs to support older AT, adding `inert` to header/main/footer on open is the belt-and-braces version. This is a policy call, not a bug.

CLAUDE.md's Known-issues line claims the 2026-07-14 fix left "axe-core: 0 color-contrast violations in both themes". That is now demonstrably scoped to the default page state only (the open listbox fails at 4.45:1 in light). Whoever fixes fs-selected-option-contrast should amend that line in the same session, per the repo's documentation discipline.

Real Cloudflare TTFB and real INP are UNANSWERABLE from this harness. server-response-time reports 0-20 ms across all runs, but that is a localhost origin - it says nothing about Cloudflare Pages edge latency. Do not report a TTFB win or loss from this audit. Likewise TBT is only a lab proxy for INP; the one page where it matters (plants-index, TBT 201 ms / maxPotentialFID 251 ms) needs field data (CrUX or a real-user metric) to know whether INP is actually poor.

Does pinning the Fraunces wght axis to 600 actually shrink the 67 KB woff2? I verified that all 8 rules consuming var(--font-display) set font-weight: 600, so the declared 100-900 axis is never exercised - but I did not inspect the font file's variation axes or run a subsetter. Verify the axes exist and measure before committing to this.

Why is hero tile index 1 the LCP rather than tile 0? I have the measurement (Lighthouse names tile 1) and a consistent explanation (equal-area LCP candidates do not replace each other, so the small lazy webp on tile 1 paints before the 102 KB JPEG on tile 0 and claims LCP), but I did not isolate the tie-break experimentally. The recommended fix - eager-load the whole first row - is robust either way, so this does not block the fix.

CLAUDE.md's Known-issues entry [ACC-1/2] claims 'axe-core: 0 color-contrast violations in both themes'. The brief's axe data shows that holds only for the DEFAULT page state - the open /plants/ listbox has a 4.45:1 selected-option label in light theme. Someone should decide whether to amend that Known-issues line, since it currently reads as an unconditional guarantee. (Contrast is the a11y auditor's lane, not mine - flagging only because it is a documented claim that the runtime data qualifies.)

Is a redesign of the card markup (sprite + gradient bloom strip) in scope for this planning round? It is the single highest-leverage change in the whole audit - it moves LCP, FCP AND TBT on the entire SEO surface - but it touches the shared card component and needs a visual regression check across hubs, listings and bloom-calendar combos.

Bloom strip and WCAG 1.4.11: is the exact bloom colour 'essential' to the visualization (which would exempt it), or is the filled-vs-empty distinction the thing that must clear 3:1? I read it as the latter, so I flagged it - but this is a design call, not a purely technical one, and the fix I propose (a hard dark ring on filled cells only) changes the look of the site's signature element on every page. Someone should sign off on the visual before it ships.

Was `outline: none` on .ht-tile (global.css:466) deliberate, and if so is the 1px border-colour substitute considered an acceptable focus indicator? It is the only divergence from the site-wide ring, and it makes hover and keyboard-focus visually identical on the 38 home-page tiles.

The sticky-header backdrop-filter cost (global.css:130) is UNMEASURED. Nobody should touch it until someone records a scroll trace on dist/plants/index.html with and without the declaration. I flagged it as a hypothesis, not a confirmed regression.

Should the .photo-credit link keep --bs-faint (5.53:1 light / 5.66:1 dark, AA) or step up to --bs-muted like the other legally-required disclosures? global.css:298, :324 and :748 all argue that a required disclosure has to be more legible than the caption token, and the CC BY attribution is exactly such a disclosure - so the code's own stated principle would put it at --bs-muted. It currently uses --bs-faint (:811).

CLAUDE.md's Known-issues line 'axe-core: 0 color-contrast violations in both themes' should be amended: it is true only for the DEFAULT page state. With the /plants/ month listbox open, the selected 'Any month' label is 4.46:1 in light theme (axe measured 4.45:1), a real AA miss.
